It is privileged and confidential, is intended solely for the internal use of Whitfield & Crane LLP and its client, and may not be disclosed to any third party (including the Company, its counsel, or any Company representative) without the prior consent of Jonathan Hsieh or Elena Marchetti.\n8\t\n9\tI. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY\n10\tThis memorandum summarizes our review of the governance documents of Terravox Systems, Inc. (“Terravox” or the “Company”) in connection with the proposed acquisition of 100% of the Company’s outstanding equity by Hargrove Capital Partners III, LP (“Hargrove” or “Buyer”), structured as a reverse triangular merger at an enterprise value of $485 million (the “Transaction”). We reviewed the Company’s Third Amended and Restated Certificate of Incorporation (the “Restated Certificate” or “Charter”), Amended and Restated Bylaws (the “Bylaws”), Stockholders’ Agreement, Voting Agreement, Registration Rights Agreement (the “RRA”), and Board minutes covering January 2023 through September 2024.\n11\tThe central finding of this diligence is that Stonemill Capital Partners Fund VI, LP (“Stonemill”) holds a hard, charter-level blocking right over the Transaction that cannot be reliably overcome through the governance documents alone. Specifically, Section 4.3.6(b)(i) of the Restated Certificate requires the affirmative consent of holders of at least sixty-six and two-thirds percent (66⅔%) of the outstanding Series C Preferred Stock, voting as a separate class, to effect any “Liquidation Event” — a threshold that, because Stonemill holds 100% of the outstanding Series C Preferred Stock (7,800,000 shares), cannot be satisfied without Stonemill’s affirmative consent. The reverse triangular merger squarely constitutes a “Liquidation Event” under Section 4.3.2(d) of the Charter. This is the decisive leverage point in the deal and the throughline of this memorandum.\n12\tKey conclusions:\n13\tNo viable path to closing over Stonemill’s objection via the governance documents. The Series C separate-class protective provision is a non-waivable charter right. The drag-along in the Stockholders’ Agreement, even if triggered, most likely cannot override this charter protective provision under Delaware law (see ODN Holding Corp. 25, LLC v. Knowlton, Del. Ch. 2011). Accordingly, the Transaction cannot be consummated without Stonemill’s consent, and the negotiation strategy must be built around obtaining it.\n14\tThe all-Preferred protective provision CAN be satisfied without Stonemill. Section 4.3.6(a)(vi) requires the consent of holders of a majority of the Preferred Stock (voting as a single class on an as-converted basis), a threshold of more than 8,250,000 of the 16,500,000 outstanding Preferred shares. Northbridge (5,200,000) and Ridgeline (3,500,000) together hold 8,700,000 shares, sufficient to satisfy this threshold without Stonemill. This narrows Stonemill’s blocking position to the Series C separate-class vote alone.\n15\tStonemill’s economic motivation to block is strong. At $485 million enterprise value (approximately $473 million equity value), the Series C liquidation preference ($144.3 million) consumes a substantial portion of the equity proceeds, and Stonemill would receive essentially its Original Issue Price back with no return, while Series B, Series A, and Common holders capture the upside. This economic structure explains Stonemill’s resistance and its likely demand for a higher allocation of consideration or a side arrangement.\n16\tA time-sensitive exclusivity issue with SensorLink Technologies may impede signing. The SensorLink LOI contains an automatic-renewal exclusivity provision; the Company’s CEO was unable to confirm whether a termination notice was sent before the September 8, 2024 expiration. If the exclusivity is still live, it could conflict with the Company’s ability to sign a definitive agreement with Hargrove. This requires immediate attention.\n17\tThe $12 million Clearfield National Bank term loan appears to have been incurred without the Preferred Stockholder consent required by Section 4.3.6(a)(v) of the Charter. The Board minutes reflect only director approval, not the separate stockholder consent the Charter requires for indebtedness exceeding $5 million. This is a governance defect that Stonemill may invoke as leverage.\n18\tThe Special Committee is not fully independent. Sandra Okafor, Northbridge’s board designee, serves on the three-member Special Committee overseeing the sale process. Her presence undermines the Committee’s independence and its cleansing effect, creating a process vulnerability that Stonemill may exploit.\n19\tCritical document gaps remain. We have not received the 2021 Equity Incentive Plan, individual award agreements, the Clearfield loan documents, the SensorLink LOI, or any stockholder consent records. Several conclusions in this memorandum are necessarily preliminary pending production of these items.\n20\tA consolidated consent/approval matrix appears in Appendix A. A document request list appears in Appendix B. Severity classifications are summarized in Appendix C.\n21\t\n22\tII. SCOPE, DOCUMENTS REVIEWED, AND CAPITALIZATION\n23\tDocuments reviewed. We reviewed the following documents, each dated November 3, 2021 (except as noted):\n24\tThird Amended and Restated Certificate of Incorporation (the “Restated Certificate” or “Charter”);\n25\tAmended and Restated Bylaws (the “Bylaws”);\n26\tStockholders’ Agreement (the “Stockholders’ Agreement”);\n27\tVoting Agreement (the “Voting Agreement”);\n28\tRegistration Rights Agreement (the “RRA”); and\n29\tBoard minutes for eight meetings spanning January 18, 2023 through September 25, 2024 (the “Board Minutes”).\n30\tTransaction structure. Hargrove will form a wholly-owned acquisition subsidiary (“Merger Sub”) that will merge with and into Terravox, with Terravox surviving as a wholly-owned subsidiary of Hargrove. All outstanding equity interests of Terravox (Common Stock and all series of Preferred Stock) will be cancelled and converted into the right to receive cash merger consideration. Enterprise value is $485 million; with approximately $12 million of outstanding indebtedness, implied equity value is approximately $473 million. Target closing is Q1 2025.\n31\tCapitalization (fully diluted, per Stockholders’ Agreement Exhibit B).\n32\t<w:tblPr><w:tblStyle w:val=\"Table\" /><w:tblW w:type=\"pct\" w:w=\"5000\" /><w:jc LP\n53\t<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">Series C Preferred\n54\t<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:rPr><w:b /><w:bCs /></w:rPr><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">100.0%\n64\tBoard composition (per Voting Agreement § 2.2). Seven directors: Marcus Ellery (CEO/Chair), Priya Chandrasekaran (Founder designee), David Kleinhans (Ridgeline/Series A designee), Sandra Okafor (Northbridge/Series B designee), Julian Trask (Stonemill/Series C designee), Rebecca Lau (Independent), and Thomas Garrett (Independent). Stonemill also holds a non-voting board observer right (Voting Agreement § 4).\n65\t\n66\tIII. AREA 1 — CHANGE-OF-CONTROL CONSENT REQUIREMENTS\n67\tTo consummate the reverse triangular merger, the following consents and approvals must be obtained. We have mapped each requirement across the Charter, the Stockholders’ Agreement, the Voting Agreement, the RRA, and the DGCL.\n68\tA. DGCL Requirements (Section 251)\n69\tUnder Section 251 of the Delaware General Corporation Law (the “DGCL”), a merger requires (i) approval of the merger agreement by the board of directors of each constituent corporation and (ii) approval by the stockholders of the surviving corporation, unless an exemption applies. The Section 251(f) exemption from stockholder approval is not available here because all outstanding shares of Terravox will be cancelled and converted into the right to receive cash — the shares do not “remain outstanding as the same share” as required by Section 251(f)(2). Accordingly, stockholder approval under Section 251(d) is required: the affirmative vote of a majority of the outstanding shares of stock entitled to vote. This is consistent with the analysis of Company counsel (Nathaniel Rourke) reflected in the September 25, 2024 Board minutes.\n70\tB. Charter Protective Provisions\n71\t1. All-Preferred protective provision (Section 4.3.6(a)(vi)). So long as any Preferred Stock is outstanding, the Company may not “effect any Liquidation Event” without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of at least a majority (greater than 50%) of the then-outstanding Preferred Stock, voting together as a single class on an as-converted basis. The Charter specifies that this requires the affirmative vote or consent of holders of more than 8,250,000 shares of Preferred Stock (based on 16,500,000 shares outstanding).\n72\tThe reverse triangular merger is a “Liquidation Event” under Section 4.3.2(d)(i) (a merger in which the Company’s stockholders do not continue to hold at least 50% of the voting power of the surviving entity) and under Section 4.3.2(d)(iii) (a transaction resulting in the transfer of more than 50% of the voting power).\n73\tThis threshold CAN be satisfied without Stonemill. Northbridge (5,200,000 shares) and Ridgeline (3,500,000 shares) together hold 8,700,000 shares, which exceeds the 8,250,000-share threshold. Stonemill’s 7,800,000 shares are not required.\n74\t2. Series C separate-class protective provision (Section 4.3.6(b)(i)). So long as any Series C Preferred Stock is outstanding, the Company may not “effect any Liquidation Event” without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of at least 66⅔% of the then-outstanding Series C Preferred Stock, voting as a separate class. The Charter specifies that this requires the affirmative vote or consent of holders of at least 5,200,000 shares of Series C (based on 7,800,000 shares outstanding).\n75\tThis threshold CANNOT be satisfied without Stonemill. Stonemill holds 100% of the outstanding Series C Preferred Stock (7,800,000 shares). Julian Trask confirmed this on the record at the September 25, 2024 Board meeting, stating that “the Series C separate class vote … cannot be satisfied without Stonemill’s affirmative consent.”\n76\tThe Section 4.3.6(b) consent is “separate from and in addition to” the Section 4.3.6(a) consent; both must be independently obtained.\n77\tC. Stockholders’ Agreement — Drag-Along (Article 3)\n78\tThe Stockholders’ Agreement contains drag-along rights that, if the “Drag-Along Approval” thresholds are met, would obligate all stockholder parties (including Stonemill) to vote in favor of a Sale of the Company. The drag-along is analyzed in detail in Section IV (Area 2). For purposes of the consent map: the drag-along is not itself a “consent” required to consummate the merger, but it is a mechanism that, if validly triggered and enforceable, could compel dissenting stockholders to vote in favor. As discussed in Section IV, the drag-along most likely cannot override the Series C separate-class protective provision.\n79\tD. Voting Agreement\n80\tThe Voting Agreement’s voting obligations (Section 3) relate to board composition, not to the merger itself. Section 3.2 expressly provides that the Voting Agreement “shall not be construed to create any obligation on the part of any Party to vote Shares in any particular manner with respect to any … proposed merger, consolidation, sale of assets, or other corporate transaction.” Accordingly, the Voting Agreement imposes no consent or voting requirement for the Transaction. The Voting Agreement terminates upon a “Sale of the Company” (Section 7.1(b)); see Section VII (Area 5).\n81\tE. Registration Rights Agreement\n82\tThe RRA does not impose a consent requirement for the merger. It terminates upon a “Deemed Liquidation Event” (Section 9(c)); see Section XI (Area 10) and Section VIII (Area 6) regarding the undefined nature of that term.\n83\tF. Third-Party Consents\n84\tClearfield National Bank term loan. The $12 million secured term loan (authorized October 11, 2023) is secured by substantially all of the Company’s assets. The loan agreement (not yet produced) may contain change-of-control covenants requiring lender consent. This must be confirmed. See Section IX (Area 8).\n85\tSensorLink Technologies LOI. The exclusivity provision of the SensorLink LOI (executed March 8, 2024) may be live and could impede signing. See Section IX (Area 8).\n86\tG. Consent Checklist\n87\tThe consolidated consent/approval matrix is set forth in Appendix A. In summary, the Transaction requires: (i) Board approval of the definitive merger agreement (not yet obtained — the September 25, 2024 resolution authorized negotiation only); (ii) a majority stockholder vote under DGCL Section 251(d) (not yet obtained); (iii) the all-Preferred consent under Section 4.3.6(a)(vi) (not yet obtained, but achievable without Stonemill); and (iv) the Series C separate-class consent under Section 4.3.6(b)(i) (not yet obtained, and not achievable without Stonemill).\n88\t\n89\tIV. AREA 2 — DRAG-ALONG ANALYSIS\n90\tA. The Three-Pronged Trigger\n91\tSection 3.1(a) of the Stockholders’ Agreement provides that a “Drag-Along Approval” is obtained only upon the satisfaction of all three of the following conditions:\n92\tCommon vote: holders of at least a majority of the outstanding shares of Common Stock (calculated on an as-converted to Common Stock basis, treating each share of Preferred Stock as converted) (Section 3.1(a)(i));\n93\tPreferred vote: holders of at least a majority of the outstanding shares of Preferred Stock (calculated on an as-converted basis, voting together as a single class) (Section 3.1(a)(ii)); and\n94\tInvestor vote: at least two (2) of the three (3) Investor Stockholders (Section 3.1(a)(iii)).\n95\tSection 3.1(b) confirms that all three conditions must be satisfied; fewer than all three does not constitute a Drag-Along Approval.\n96\tB. Can the Drag-Along Be Triggered Without Stonemill?\n97\tCondition (ii) — Preferred vote: The threshold is a majority of the 16,500,000 outstanding Preferred shares (as-converted), i.e., more than 8,250,000 shares. Without Stonemill, Northbridge (5,200,000) and Ridgeline (3,500,000) together hold 8,700,000 shares — sufficient to satisfy this condition. Satisfiable without Stonemill.\n98\tCondition (iii) — Investor vote: At least two of the three Investor Stockholders (Ridgeline, Northbridge, Stonemill) must approve. Without Stonemill, both Ridgeline and Northbridge must approve. Satisfiable without Stonemill (assuming both Ridgeline and Northbridge consent, which is consistent with their expressed support in the Board minutes).\n99\tCondition (i) — Common vote: This condition is the principal uncertainty. The provision requires a majority of the outstanding Common Stock “calculated on an as-converted to Common Stock basis, treating each share of Preferred Stock as having been converted.” Read naturally, this is a majority of the fully diluted as-converted base. On a fully diluted basis of approximately 33,900,000 shares, a majority requires more than approximately 16,950,000 shares. The non-Stonemill parties to the Stockholders’ Agreement (the Founders, Ridgeline, and Northbridge) collectively hold 16,000,000 as-converted shares (Founders 7,300,000 + Ridgeline 3,500,000 + Northbridge 5,200,000) — approximately 950,000 shares short of the threshold. Satisfaction of this condition without Stonemill would therefore require the support of additional Common holders who are not parties to the Stockholders’ Agreement (a portion of the 10,100,000 shares held by “Various Common Holders / Option Pool”). The availability of that support cannot be confirmed without the complete capitalization table and clarification of whether unexercised options and unvested RSUs are counted as “outstanding shares of Common Stock.” Uncertain — likely requires non-party Common holder support.\n100\tPreliminary conclusion on trigger: Conditions (ii) and (iii) can be satisfied without Stonemill. Condition (i) is uncertain and may require support from non-party Common holders. Even if all three conditions are satisfied, however, the more fundamental question is whether the drag-along can override the Charter’s Series C protective provision, addressed below.\n101\tC. Can the Drag-Along Override the Charter Protective Provisions?\n102\tThis is the critical legal question. If the drag-along is validly triggered, Section 3.1(a) obligates each Restricted Stockholder — including Stonemill — to vote all of its Shares (including its Series C Preferred Stock) in favor of the Sale of the Company. Section 3.3 reinforces this with an irrevocable proxy granted to the holders constituting the Drag-Along Approval. If Stonemill were compelled to vote 100% of its Series C in favor, the 66⅔% Series C separate-class threshold would be satisfied.\n103\tThe difficulty is that the Series C separate-class vote is a protective provision embedded in the Charter — the supreme governance document of the Company. Under Delaware law, a stockholders’ agreement is a contract among stockholders and cannot amend or override the certificate of incorporation. The leading authority is ODN Holding Corp. 25, LLC v. Knowlton, 2011 WL 6153080 (Del. Ch. Dec. 2, 2011), in which the Court of Chancery held that a drag-along provision could not be used to circumvent the class-voting protections of the certificate of incorporation. The court reasoned that the certificate of incorporation is the supreme governing document and that a stockholders’ agreement cannot be employed to override its protective provisions or to force a transaction that violates a charter class-vote requirement.\n104\tThere is a colorable counter-argument: the drag-along does not “amend” the Charter but rather compels a stockholder to vote in a particular way, and if the compelled vote satisfies the Charter threshold, no Charter provision is violated. On this view, Stonemill contractually agreed to be dragged and must vote accordingly. The irrevocable proxy in Section 3.3 strengthens the enforcement argument. However, this argument is in tension with the ODN reasoning and has not been reliably endorsed by Delaware courts as a means to override a separate-class protective provision to which the protected holder objects.\n105\tAssessment. This is a genuinely contested question under Delaware law. The weight of authority and the ODN line of cases suggest that the drag-along most likely cannot override the Series C separate-class protective provision. Relying on the drag-along to close over Stonemill’s objection would expose the Transaction to significant litigation risk, including a likely injunction motion by Stonemill seeking to enjoin the closing on the ground that the Series C separate-class consent was not validly obtained. The outcome of such litigation is uncertain and would likely delay or derail the Transaction.\n106\tRecommendation. Do not rely on the drag-along as a primary path to closing over Stonemill’s objection. Treat the Series C separate-class vote as a hard blocking right requiring Stonemill’s affirmative consent. The drag-along may be retained as a secondary negotiating lever and a potential litigation fallback, but it should not be the cornerstone of the closing strategy.\n107\tD. Drag-Along Terms Affecting Consideration\n108\tSection 3.2(a) provides that each Restricted Stockholder receives the same form and amount of consideration per share (as-converted), subject to the liquidation waterfall of the Charter — i.e., consideration is distributed “as if the Sale of the Company were a Liquidation Event.” Section 3.2(d) limits stockholder representations and warranties to title, authority, and enforceability, and limits indemnification to a several (not joint) basis capped at the consideration received; no stockholder may be required to enter non-competition or non-solicitation covenants. These terms are favorable to the selling stockholders and would, if the drag-along is invoked, govern the stockholders’ obligations in the merger agreement.\n109\t\n110\tV. AREA 3 — IMPEDIMENTS TO CLOSING A REVERSE TRIANGULAR MERGER\n111\tA. Defined Terms Clearly Capture the Structure\n112\tThe reverse triangular merger is unambiguously captured by the operative defined terms:\n113\t“Liquidation Event” (Charter § 4.3.2(d)(i)): “any merger, consolidation, or other business combination of the Corporation with or into another entity (whether or not the Corporation is the surviving entity), other than [one in which the stockholders retain at least 50% of the voting power].” The explicit “whether or not the Corporation is the surviving entity” language confirms that both forward and reverse mergers are captured. Because Terravox stockholders will retain 0% of the voting power post-merger (Hargrove will own 100%), the Transaction is a Liquidation Event. Section 4.3.2(d)(iii) (transfer of more than 50% of voting power) is independently satisfied.\n114\t“Sale of the Company” (Stockholders’ Agreement § 1.1; Voting Agreement § 1): “any merger, consolidation, or reorganization of the Company in which the stockholders of the Company immediately prior to such transaction do not retain a majority of the voting power of the surviving or resulting entity.” The Transaction satisfies this definition.\n115\tAccordingly, there is no structural ambiguity as to whether the reverse triangular merger triggers the Liquidation Event and Sale of the Company definitions. The consent and termination consequences follow.\n116\tB. DGCL Section 251(f) Does Not Apply\n117\tAs noted in Section III, because all Terravox shares will be cancelled and converted into cash, the Section 251(f) exemption from stockholder approval is unavailable, and a full stockholder vote under Section 251(d) is required. This is consistent with the structure (a 100% cash-out acquisition) and with Company counsel’s analysis.\n118\tC. Survival of the Company and Its Contracts\n119\tIn a reverse triangular merger, Terravox survives as a wholly-owned subsidiary. This has two principal implications:\n120\tContracts generally survive without assignment. Because the legal entity persists, contracts that would otherwise require counterparty consent upon an assignment or change of control by merger may not be triggered (depending on the specific change-of-control language in each contract). This is generally an advantage of the reverse triangular structure. However, certain contracts contain change-of-control triggers that apply regardless of entity survival — most notably the Clearfield term loan (see Section IX) and potentially the Permian Basin Energy Co. master services agreement and other commercial contracts. We have not yet reviewed these contracts and cannot confirm whether change-of-control consents are required.\n121\tCharter and Bylaws survive (as subsidiary documents). The Charter and Bylaws remain in effect post-closing until amended. The protective provisions become practically moot because no Preferred Stock will remain outstanding post-closing, but they must be satisfied at closing (before the shares are cancelled). The Series C separate-class consent is therefore a closing condition, not a post-closing concern.\n122\tD. The RRA “Deemed Liquidation Event” Gap\n123\tThe RRA terminates upon “a Deemed Liquidation Event” (Section 9(c)), but the RRA does not define “Deemed Liquidation Event.” The Charter defines “Liquidation Event” (not “Deemed Liquidation Event”), and the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement define “Sale of the Company.” The absence of a defined term in the RRA creates ambiguity as to whether the Transaction terminates the RRA. This is addressed in detail in Sections VIII (Area 6) and XI (Area 10). The merger agreement should include an express termination/waiver of the RRA at closing to eliminate this ambiguity.\n124\tE. Conclusion on Structure\n125\tThe reverse triangular merger structure does not create unique definitional impediments — the operative defined terms clearly capture it. The principal structure-related risks are (i) the survival of the Company’s contracts (requiring review for change-of-control consents, particularly the Clearfield loan) and (ii) the RRA definitional gap. Neither is a deal-breaker, but both require affirmative attention in the merger agreement and diligence.\n126\t\n127\tVI. AREA 4 — TRANSFER RESTRICTIONS\n128\tA. ROFR and Co-Sale (Stockholders’ Agreement §§ 2.2–2.3)\n129\tSection 2.1 prohibits any Restricted Stockholder from Transferring Shares except (a) in compliance with Article 2, (b) pursuant to a Permitted Transfer (Section 2.7), or (c) “in connection with a Sale of the Company consummated in accordance with Article 3.” Section 2.7(e) lists as a Permitted Transfer any “Transfer[] in connection with a Sale of the Company consummated in accordance with Article 3.”\n130\tThe right of first refusal (Section 2.2) is triggered when a Restricted Stockholder “receives a bona fide written offer from a third party … to purchase any Shares and desires to accept such offer.” The co-sale right (Section 2.3) is triggered when the Company and other Restricted Stockholders do not purchase all Shares subject to a ROFR Notice. These provisions are designed for individual stockholder sales to third parties, not for a statutory merger in which all shares are cancelled and converted by operation of law.\n131\tWhether the merger triggers ROFR/co-sale turns on the conditional carve-out. The Sale of the Company carve-out in Sections 2.1(c) and 2.7(e) is conditioned on the Sale of the Company being “consummated in accordance with Article 3” — i.e., pursuant to the drag-along. If the drag-along is invoked and the Sale of the Company is consummated under Article 3, the merger is a Permitted Transfer and ROFR/co-sale do not apply. If the drag-along is not invoked (for example, because the Transaction proceeds on the basis of stockholder consents and a stockholder vote without a formal drag-along trigger), the conditional language creates ambiguity as to whether the merger falls within the carve-out.\n132\tPractical resolution. The Stockholders’ Agreement terminates upon “a Sale of the Company” (Section 6.1(b)), so the transfer restrictions cease upon closing in any event. The concern is whether the restrictions impede the path to closing. Because the merger is a statutory transaction (not a voluntary sale by an individual stockholder) and the Stockholders’ Agreement terminates upon the Sale of the Company, the better view is that ROFR/co-sale do not apply to the merger. Nonetheless, the conditional carve-out language is imprecise.\n133\tRecommendation. Obtain an interpretive confirmation or waiver from the stockholder parties (or structure the Transaction to invoke the drag-along formally) to eliminate any argument that ROFR or co-sale rights are triggered. The merger agreement should include a representation that the Transaction constitutes a Sale of the Company and that the transfer restrictions do not apply.\n134\tB. Tag-Along Rights (Section 2.4)\n135\tTag-along rights apply to a “Founder Transfer” — a Founder’s transfer of more than 5% of the outstanding Shares to a non-Affiliate third party. The merger is not a Founder Transfer in the ordinary sense; it is a Sale of the Company. The same analysis as ROFR/co-sale applies: the Sale of the Company carve-out and the termination of the Stockholders’ Agreement upon a Sale of the Company (Section 6.1(b)) should govern. No separate impediment is identified, but the conditional-carve-out ambiguity noted above applies equally here.\n136\tC. Founder Lock-Up (Section 2.5)\n137\tThe 12-month lock-up period ran from November 3, 2021 and expired on November 3, 2022. It is no longer in effect and presents no impediment.\n138\tD. Voting Agreement Transfer Restriction (Section 6.2)\n139\tSection 6.2 of the Voting Agreement prohibits Transfers unless the transferee agrees to be bound by the Voting Agreement. The Voting Agreement terminates upon a Sale of the Company (Section 7.1(b)), so this restriction ceases upon closing. The merger is not a voluntary Transfer by a party in the conventional sense. No impediment is identified.\n140\tE. Conclusion on Transfer Restrictions\n141\tThe transfer restrictions should not impede the Transaction, particularly given that the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement both terminate upon a Sale of the Company. The principal risk is the conditional carve-out language (“consummated in accordance with Article 3”), which creates ambiguity if the drag-along is not formally invoked. We recommend addressing this through waiver/confirmation or formal drag-along invocation, and through appropriate provisions in the merger agreement.\n142\t\n143\tVII. AREA 5 — BOARD COMPOSITION: POST-CLOSING IMPLICATIONS\n144\tA. Termination of the Voting Agreement Upon the Merger\n145\tSection 7.1 of the Voting Agreement provides that the agreement terminates upon the earliest of: (a) an IPO; (b) “the consummation of a Sale of the Company (as defined in Section 1)”; (c) written consent of the Company and specified stockholder majorities; or (d) November 3, 2026 (the fifth anniversary).\n146\tThe “Sale of the Company” definition (Section 1) captures the reverse triangular merger (a merger in which the stockholders do not retain a majority of the voting power of the surviving entity). Accordingly, the Voting Agreement terminates upon closing of the Transaction. The board designation rights in Section 2.2 lapse at closing; they do not survive, and the November 3, 2026 sunset is moot if the Transaction closes before that date (target Q1 2025).\n147\tPost-closing board. Upon closing, Hargrove, as the sole stockholder of the surviving subsidiary, will control the board and may appoint directors as it sees fit. No designation rights, voting obligations, or committee-composition requirements under the Voting Agreement survive. This is a clean outcome.\n148\tB. Stonemill’s Board Observer Right (Section 4)\n149\tSection 4.1 grants Stonemill the right to appoint a non-voting observer to attend all Board (and committee) meetings and to receive all Board materials, for so long as Stonemill holds at least 1,000,000 shares of Series C Preferred Stock (or Common issued upon conversion). Section 7.3 provides that the observer right terminates if Stonemill ceases to hold the minimum share threshold.\n150\tUpon closing, all Series C Preferred Stock is cancelled and converted into cash, so Stonemill ceases to hold any Series C (or Common issued upon conversion). The observer right therefore terminates at closing. In addition, the Voting Agreement itself terminates upon the Sale of the Company (Section 7.1(b)), independently terminating the observer right. The observer right does not survive the Transaction.\n151\tDuring the signing-to-closing period, however, the observer right is live. Stonemill’s observer is entitled to attend Board meetings and receive Board materials during the pendency of the Transaction. This is significant because the Board will be considering the merger agreement and related matters. Section 4.2 permits the Company to exclude the observer (and withhold materials) to preserve attorney-client privilege or to address a bona fide conflict of interest between Stonemill and the Company, upon advice of counsel. Given Stonemill’s adversarial posture, the Company should be prepared to invoke this exclusion judiciously for privileged deliberations and conflict-sensitive matters. We note that the Stonemill observer was not present at the April 17, 2024 meeting at which the Board authorized engagement with Hargrove — a fact relevant to the sale-process analysis in Section X (Area 9).\n152\tC. Committee Composition (Section 2.4)\n153\tSection 2.4 provides that, unless otherwise agreed by the Investor Stockholders, each Board committee must include at least one Investor Stockholder designee. This requirement is live during the signing-to-closing period. The Special Committee currently includes Sandra Okafor (Northbridge’s designee), satisfying this requirement. Post-closing, the requirement is moot. The composition of the Special Committee is analyzed in Section X (Area 9).\n154\tD. Conclusion on Board Composition\n155\tThe Voting Agreement and its designation rights and observer rights terminate cleanly upon closing. No post-closing governance entanglement arises from the Voting Agreement. The principal operational concern is managing Stonemill’s observer rights (and information access) during the signing-to-closing period.\n156\t\n157\tVIII. AREA 6 — CROSS-DOCUMENT DEFINITIONAL INCONSISTISTENCIES\n158\tWe compared the key defined terms across all five governance documents. The most significant findings are as follows.\n159\tA. “Deemed Liquidation Event” (RRA) Is Undefined — Critical Gap\n160\tThe RRA terminates upon “a Deemed Liquidation Event” (Section 9(c)) but does not define “Deemed Liquidation Event” anywhere in the agreement (the defined terms in Section 1 do not include it). The Charter defines “Liquidation Event” (Section 4.3.2(d)); the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement define “Sale of the Company” (each in Section 1). The RRA does not cross-reference the Charter’s “Liquidation Event” definition.\n161\tThe evident intent is that “Deemed Liquidation Event” corresponds to the Charter’s “Liquidation Event” (which captures the merger). However, because the term is undefined, a strict reading creates ambiguity as to whether the Transaction terminates the RRA. This is the most significant cross-document inconsistency and has direct consequences for the RRA’s post-transaction treatment (Section XI, Area 10). Severity: Significant. The merger agreement should include an express termination/waiver of the RRA at closing.\n162\tB. Threshold Mismatch: “At Least 50%” (Charter) vs. “Majority” (Stockholders’ Agreement / Voting Agreement)\n163\tCharter § 4.3.2(d)(i): a merger is a Liquidation Event unless the Company’s stockholders “continue to hold at least fifty percent (50%)” of the voting power of the surviving entity.\n164\tStockholders’ Agreement § 1.1 / Voting Agreement § 1: a merger is a Sale of the Company if the stockholders “do not retain a majority” of the voting power of the surviving entity.\n165\t“At least 50%” (≥50%) and “majority” (>50%) differ at the boundary: if the stockholders retained exactly 50% of the voting power, the transaction would not be a Liquidation Event under the Charter (50% ≥ 50%) but would be a Sale of the Company under the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement (50% is not a majority, so the stockholders do not “retain a majority”). For the Hargrove Transaction (stockholders retain 0%), both definitions are triggered, so there is no practical effect here. The inconsistency is nonetheless a drafting defect that could produce divergent outcomes in other transactions (e.g., a minority investment or a partial roll-over structure). Severity: Moderate.\n166\tC. Equity Financing Carve-Out Present in Charter, Absent Elsewhere\n167\tCharter § 4.3.2(d)(iii): excludes from the Liquidation Event definition “an equity financing transaction primarily for capital-raising purposes.”\n168\tStockholders’ Agreement § 1.1 / Voting Agreement § 1: contain no equivalent equity-financing carve-out in their Sale of the Company definitions.\n169\tAccordingly, an equity financing that results in the transfer of more than 50% of the voting power would be a Sale of the Company (triggering drag-along, termination, etc.) under the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement but would not be a Liquidation Event under the Charter (and thus would not trigger the liquidation preference or the protective provisions). This divergence is not implicated by the Hargrove Transaction (which is not an equity financing) but is a latent inconsistency. Severity: Moderate.\n170\tD. IPO Definition Inconsistencies\n171\tCharter § 4.3.3(b)(i): mandatory conversion upon an IPO with aggregate gross proceeds of at least $75,000,000 and a price per share of at least $55.50 (3x the Series C Original Issue Price).\n172\tStockholders’ Agreement § 6.1(a): termination upon an IPO with gross proceeds of at least $75,000,000 (no per-share price threshold).\n173\tVoting Agreement § 7.1(a): termination upon an IPO with gross proceeds of at least $75,000,000 (no per-share price threshold).\n174\tRRA § 1: “IPO” means the Company’s first underwritten public offering of Common Stock (no proceeds threshold).\n175\tThe Charter’s per-share price threshold ($55.50) is unique to the Charter and affects only the timing of mandatory conversion of Preferred Stock. The divergence is not implicated by the Transaction but is a latent inconsistency affecting when the various agreements would terminate in an IPO scenario. Severity: Moderate.\n176\tE. Other Minor Inconsistencies\n177\tThe Charter’s Liquidation Event asset-disposition prong (Section 4.3.2(d)(ii)) uses “sale, lease, transfer, exclusive license, or other disposition,” while the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement use “sale, lease, exclusive license, or other disposition” (omitting “transfer”). Substantively similar; minor drafting variance.\n178\tThe Charter uses “merger, consolidation, or other business combination” (Section 4.3.2(d)(i)); the Stockholders’ Agreement and Voting Agreement use “merger, consolidation, or reorganization.” Substantively similar; minor drafting variance.\n179\t“Affiliate” is defined only in the Stockholders’ Agreement (Section 1.1); the Voting Agreement and RRA use the concept without defining it.\n180\tF. Document Completeness Question\n181\tThe preamble to the Board Minutes refers to “the Investors’ Rights Agreement, the Voting Agreement, the Right of First Refusal and Co-Sale Agreement, and the Stockholders’ Agreement (collectively, the ‘Governance Agreements’)” — listing four agreements. We have received three (the Stockholders’ Agreement, the Voting Agreement, and the RRA). It appears that the Stockholders’ Agreement combines the ROFR/co-sale and information-rights functions (Articles 2 and 4) and the RRA provides registration rights, such that the “Investors’ Rights Agreement” and “Right of First Refusal and Co-Sale Agreement” referenced in the minutes may be informal names for the documents we have. We should confirm with Company counsel that we have received all operative governance agreements and that no separate Investors’ Rights Agreement or ROFR/Co-Sale Agreement exists. Severity: Moderate (document completeness).\n182\t\n183\tIX. AREA 7 — OUTSTANDING EQUITY AWARDS AND CHANGE-OF-CONTROL ACCELERATION\n184\tA. Document Gap — Critical\n185\tWe have not received the 2021 Equity Incentive Plan (the “Plan”) or any individual award agreements (option agreements, RSU agreements). Without these documents, we cannot determine (i) whether the merger constitutes a “change of control” under the Plan, (ii) whether unvested awards accelerate on a single-trigger or double-trigger basis, (iii) whether awards are assumed, cashed out, or cancelled in the merger, or (iv) the aggregate dollar impact of any acceleration. The Plan and all award agreements must be requested immediately. Severity: Critical (document gap).\n186\tB. Awards Identified in the Board Minutes\n187\tRaj Malhotra (VP of Engineering): 350,000 stock options, exercise price $14.30 per share, four-year vesting with a one-year cliff, granted April 12, 2023 under the Plan.\n188\tLinda Yeung (CFO): 200,000 RSUs, three-year vesting (one-third per year), granted April 17, 2024 under the Plan (as amended).\n189\tAdditional awards are presumably outstanding to other employees, directors, and consultants under the Plan; the “Various Common Holders / Option Pool” line (10,100,000 shares) in the capitalization table includes a mix of issued Common Stock, outstanding options, and RSUs, the precise composition of which is unknown.\n190\tC. Preliminary Waterfall and Per-Share Consideration Analysis\n191\tBased on the figures in the September 25, 2024 Board minutes, we performed a preliminary liquidation waterfall analysis at the $473 /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">3,500,000\n212\t<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">$14,000,000 ($4.00/sh)\n213\t<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">Converts (as-converted > preference)\n214\t<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">~$12.59\n215\t<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">~$44,100,000\n216\t<w:tc><w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">Common (incl. options/RSUs)\n217\t<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">17,400,000\n218\t<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">—\n219\t<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">Residual\n220\t<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">~$12.59\n221\t<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">~$219,100,000\n222\tMethodology: Series C takes its $18.50/share liquidation preference ($144.3 million) because converting would yield only approximately $13.95/share on a pro rata basis across all 33,900,000 fully diluted shares — less than the preference. The remaining approximately $328.7 million is shared pro rata among Series B, Series A, and Common (26,100,000 shares), yielding approximately $12.59/share. Series B and Series A each convert because $12.59/share exceeds their respective preferences ($9.25 and $4.00).\n223\tThis analysis reveals two important points:\n224\tThe September 25, 2024 Board minutes’ characterization appears imprecise. The minutes state that “all holders of Preferred Stock would receive amounts in excess of their respective liquidation preferences on an as-converted basis, suggesting that participation on an as-converted basis would be economically favorable for all preferred holders.” In fact, Series C does not receive an amount in excess of its liquidation preference — it receives exactly its preference ($18.50/share) and does not participate on an as-converted basis (because as-converted participation would yield less). Only Series B and Series A receive amounts in excess of their preferences. This imprecision in the Board’s characterization should be noted; it may reflect an oversimplification in the minutes or in Meridian’s preliminary analysis, and it is relevant to the fairness analysis (Section X, Area 9).\n225\tStonemill’s economic position explains its resistance. At $485 million enterprise value, Stonemill receives approximately $144.3 million — essentially a return of its Original Issue Price ($18.50 × 7,800,000), with no gain. Meanwhile, Northbridge, Ridgeline, and the Common holders capture the upside above the preferences. This is the economic root of Stonemill’s opposition: the Series C liquidation preference is so large relative to the equity value that Stonemill is, in effect, the “last money in” bearing the risk with no upside at this price. Stonemill’s likely objective — a higher allocation of consideration or a side arrangement — is economically rational from its perspective and should inform the negotiation strategy.\n226\tD. Impact on Equity Awards\n227\tMalhotra options (350,000 @ $14.30): The implied Common per-share consideration (~$12.59) is below the $14.30 exercise price, meaning these options are currently underwater. If the options are cashed out at closing on a single-trigger basis, the net value would be zero (or the options would be cancelled for no consideration). However, this depends on the final per-share consideration (which depends on the final equity value and waterfall) and the Plan’s treatment of underwater options. If the deal value increases, the options could move into the money.\n228\tYeung RSUs (200,000): RSUs have no exercise price. If single-trigger accelerated, 200,000 RSUs × ~$12.59/share ≈ $2.5 million. As of a Q1 2025 closing, only approximately one-third would be vested absent acceleration.\n229\tAggregate impact: The aggregate acceleration cost appears modest based on the identified awards, but the full option pool (a portion of the 10,100,000 shares) is unquantified. The Plan and complete award schedule are required to assess the total impact.\n230\tE. Equity Plan Share Reserve Increase (July 2023) — Stockholder Approval Question\n231\tAt the July 19, 2023 Board meeting, the Board approved an amendment to the Plan increasing the share reserve from 8,000,000 to 9,500,000 shares (a 1,500,000-share increase). The minutes reflect that outside counsel (Mr. Rourke) “noted that the amendment required Board approval,” and the Board approved the increase 7-0. The minutes do not reflect any stockholder approval.\n232\tWhether stockholder approval was required depends on the Plan’s terms (which we have not received). Under Delaware law, increasing a plan’s share reserve does not increase the authorized capital stock (and thus does not trigger the Charter’s Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) protective provision, which concerns authorized shares). However, stockholder approval may be required (i) if the Plan so provides, (ii) to maintain incentive stock option (“ISO”) qualification under Section 422 of the Internal Revenue Code (if the Plan grants ISOs), or (iii) for other tax or securities-law reasons. As a private company, exchange-listing standards (e.g., the 20% rule) do not apply. We must review the Plan to determine whether stockholder approval was required and, if so, whether it was obtained. Severity: Moderate (pending Plan review).\n233\tThe anti-dilution provisions of the Charter (Section 4.3.4(b)(i)) exempt shares issued “pursuant to equity incentive plans or stock purchase plans approved by the Board of Directors, including at least one director elected by the holders of Preferred Stock.” The Board includes Preferred-designated directors (Messrs. Kleinhans and Trask and Ms. Okafor), so grants under the Plan are exempt from anti-dilution adjustment. No anti-dilution issue is presented by the reserve increase or the grants.\n234\t\n235\tX. AREA 8 — AUTHORIZATION OF CORPORATE ACTIONS: BOARD MINUTES REVIEW\n236\tWe reviewed all eight sets of Board minutes (January 18, 2023 through September 25, 2024). For each significant corporate action, we assessed whether proper Board approval was obtained and whether stockholder or Preferred Stockholder consent was required and, if so, whether it was obtained.\n237\tA. Meeting-by-Meeting Summary\n238\tMeeting 1 — January 18, 2023 (7/7 present; quorum satisfied). Approved the FY2023 operating budget; authorized the Permian Basin Energy Co. master services agreement (up to $15 million); and authorized the engagement of Meridian Partners LLC as financial advisor to explore strategic alternatives. All actions approved 7-0. No protective provision implicated. Status: Not yet obtained (pending definitive documentation).\n115:We note for completeness that several other Section 4.3.6(a) protective provisions are not directly triggered by the merger itself but are relevant to related actions: Section 4.3.6(a)(i) (charter/bylaw amendments adversely affecting Preferred — relevant if the merger agreement requires a charter amendment); Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) (increasing/decreasing authorized shares); and Section 4.3.6(a)(vii) (increasing/decreasing Board size). The merger as structured (cancellation of all equity for cash) does not require a charter amendment, so these are not independently triggered, but we should confirm that the merger agreement does not contemplate any pre-closing charter amendment that would independently require Preferred consent.\n253:Charter protective provisions. Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) requires Preferred consent to “increase or decrease the total number of authorized shares of Common Stock or Preferred Stock, or any series thereof.” The share-reserve increase does not increase the authorized Common Stock (50,000,000 authorized, unchanged); it merely reserves additional authorized shares for issuance under the Plan. Accordingly, Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) is likely not triggered. However, the anti-dilution exception in Section 4.3.4(b)(i) exempts options issued under a Board-approved plan (including at least one Preferred-designated director) from anti-dilution adjustment, so the increase should not trigger Preferred anti-dilution. We nonetheless recommend confirming that no other protective provision (e.g., a plan-specific consent right in a separate Investors’ Rights Agreement, if one exists — see Section VIII.C) was implicated.\n269:January 24, 2024: The Board authorized the CEO to negotiate and execute a non-binding LOI for a potential acquisition of SensorLink for up to $22,000,000 (7-0).\n281:Strategic alternatives process (Jan. 2023 – present): The Board authorized engagement of Meridian Partners (Jan. 18, 2023), formed the Special Committee (April 12, 2023), pursued exclusivity with “Beta” (July 19, 2023, approved 6-1, Trask dissenting — Trask objected the process was “insufficiently broad”), terminated Beta exclusivity (Oct. 11, 2023), received inbound Hargrove interest (April 17, 2024), granted Hargrove exclusivity (July 10, 2024, 6-1, Trask dissenting), and authorized definitive-documentation negotiation (Sept. 25, 2024, 6-1, Trask dissenting). Stonemill’s observer is entitled to attend Board meetings and receive Board materials during the pendency of the Transaction. This is significant because the Board will be considering the merger agreement and related matters. Section 4.2 permits the Company to exclude the observer (and withhold materials) to preserve attorney-client privilege or to address a bona fide conflict of interest between Stonemill and the Company, upon advice of counsel. Given Stonemill’s adversarial posture, the Company should be prepared to invoke this exclusion judiciously for privileged deliberations and conflict-sensitive matters. Under Delaware law, increasing a plan’s share reserve does not increase the authorized capital stock (and thus does not trigger the Charter’s Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) protective provision, which concerns authorized shares). However, stockholder approval may be required (i) if the Plan so provides, (ii) to maintain incentive stock option (“ISO”) qualification under Section 422 of the Internal Revenue Code (if the Plan grants ISOs), or (iii) for other tax or securities-law reasons. As a private company, exchange-listing standards (e.g., the 20% rule) do not apply. We must review the Plan to determine whether stockholder approval was required and, if so, whether it was obtained. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-opus-4-8","id":"msg_01D5g8eTb7ndfEHT7WyrDz6c","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"toolu_011mLLSj55L8CRrLzC79wRcE","name":"Bash","input":{"command":"cd /tmp\necho \"===================== C-001/C-002 EBITDA ERROR & VALUATION IMPACT =====================\"\necho \"--- ITER0 (passed both) ---\"\ngrep -niE \"16\\.8|17\\.1|\\\\\\$0\\.3M|0\\.3M|2\\.4M|3\\.0M|arithmet|foot|do not foot|discrepanc\" /tmp/iter0_text.txt | head -40\necho \"\"\necho \"--- ITER1 (failed both) ---\"\ngrep -niE \"16\\.8|17\\.1|\\\\\\$0\\.3M|0\\.3M|2\\.4M|3\\.0M|arithmet|foot|do not foot|discrepanc\" /tmp/iter1_text.txt | head -40","description":"Compare EBITDA arithmetic error coverage"},"caller":{"type":"direct"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"stop_details":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":131,"cache_creation_input_tokens":819,"cache_read_input_tokens":33462,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":819,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":2,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"global"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_0156rfzvU6zUanWCLDMqZ2qf","session_id":"ad004501-7372-4a84-90be-02a36df6419c","uuid":"0b9ee2d9-ee5f-49fc-a4c1-ce15de18a9c8","request_id":"req_011CcUVQrn8mGcm5dUXvwpVV","subagent_type":"general-purpose","task_description":"Analyze cim-teaser regression"}
{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_011mLLSj55L8CRrLzC79wRcE","type":"tool_result","content":"===================== C-001/C-002 EBITDA ERROR & VALUATION IMPACT =====================\n--- ITER0 (passed both) ---\n21:Recommendation: Proceed to submit a non-binding IOI, but at a valuation meaningfully below the sell-side guidance and subject to explicit conditions precedent. CES is a genuinely attractive Pacific Northwest environmental services platform that fits Fund III’s sector mandate, and the regulatory tailwinds (PFAS, enforcement) and recurring-revenue characteristics are real. However, the CIM’s headline economics are flattered by (i) an arithmetic error in the Adjusted EBITDA bridge, (ii) EBITDA add-backs that are not defensible against the CIM’s own market data and sector benchmarks, (iii) financial exhibits that do not reconcile to the CIM or to themselves, (iv) a customer-concentration profile whose largest relationship is on an expired master service agreement, and (v) a growth plan that is almost entirely dependent on a two-year-old service line growing at roughly three times the rate sector experience supports. These issues are addressable through diligence, but they materially affect price, structure, and conditionality.\n22:Headline valuation gap. The CIM guides to an enterprise value of $136.8M–$171.0M (8.0x–10.0x FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA of $17.1M). Our buy-side normalized FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA is ~$15.9M (a ~$1.2M / 7% reduction), implying an EV of ~$127M–$159M at 8.0x–10.0x, and ~$143M at a 9.0x midpoint. A more conservative case (further owner-compensation haircut and exhibits-implied EBITDA) supports ~$15.0M of Adjusted EBITDA and an EV of ~$120M–$150M. We recommend an IOI enterprise-value range of $130M–$150M (cash-free, debt-free, normalized NWC), with a strong preference for the lower half pending diligence, and an earnout tied to PFAS revenue and the PNR contract renewal.\n24:Adjusted EBITDA does not foot. The six stated adjustments sum to $4.7M, which when added to reported EBITDA of $12.1M yields $16.8M, not the $17.1M presented. The $0.3M gap is an arithmetic error that, at an 8.0x–10.0x multiple, represents $2.4M–$3.0M of enterprise value on its own.\n33:FY2024 profile (as presented). Revenue $87.3M (12.5% CAGR FY2021–FY2024); reported EBITDA $12.1M (13.9% margin); Adjusted EBITDA $17.1M (19.6% margin); contracted backlog $42.6M; pipeline $31.4M; 150+ active customers.\n100:This is the most important financial issue. The CIM lifts reported EBITDA of $12.1M to Adjusted EBITDA of $17.1M via $5.0M of implied adjustments. The bridge does not withstand scrutiny on three counts.\n101:(1) The bridge does not foot (arithmetic error). The six stated adjustments sum to $4.7M, not $5.0M. Reported EBITDA $12.1M + $4.7M = $16.8M, yet the CIM and the EBITDA Bridge tab both state $17.1M. The EBITDA Bridge tab is internally inconsistent: it lists “Total Adjustments 4,700” and “Adjusted EBITDA 17,100” in adjacent rows. The $0.3M overstatement is worth $2.4M–$3.0M of EV at 8.0x–10.0x before any other adjustment.\n103:(3) The regulatory/legal add-back treats a recurring cost as one-time. The $1.2M add-back ($875K DEQ settlement + $325K legal fees, August 14, 2024) is presented as fully non-recurring. Sector benchmarks and Thornfield’s portfolio experience say otherwise: environmental-services companies of this size incur $0.2M–$0.5M per year of recurring regulatory/compliance cost (routine defense, permit renewals, audits, minor enforcement responses, fines). At GreenWorks, a “$380K one-time” add-back was followed by ~$275K/year of recurring regulatory expense; at Allied Waste, recurring regulatory costs averaged ~$310K/year with a spike to $840K. Best-practice QoE normalizes to a run-rate and adds back only the excess. Assuming a $0.3M–$0.5M run-rate, the defensible add-back is $0.7M–$0.9M, an overstatement of $0.3M–$0.5M ($2.4M–$5.0M of EV). This requires CES’s full 10-year enforcement history to confirm the DEQ matter is isolated rather than systemic — particularly because it involved improper storage of contaminated soils at the Portland facility, an operational compliance failure at the core site.\n105:Owner compensation normalization ($1.9M). $2.4M total comp (including “personal expenses allocated to the Company”) less a $0.5M replacement-CEO assumption. The personal-expense component must be substantiated as truly personal. The $0.5M replacement assumption may be light: a PE-licensed environmental-services CEO in the Pacific Northwest market could plausibly cost $0.75M–$1.0M total, which would reduce the defensible add-back to $1.4M–$1.65M.\n106:Phantom-unit stock-based compensation ($0.3M). The non-cash P&L expense is a reasonable add-back, but all outstanding phantom units settle in cash at closing. That cash settlement is a real transaction/retention cost that is not captured in the add-back and must be sized and allocated between purchase price and closing/retention costs.\n146:17.1\n149:Memo: arithmetic correction to CIM\n153:The buy-side figure of ~$15.9M is ~7% below the CIM’s $17.1M. A conservative case that also (a) uses the exhibits-implied reported EBITDA of ~$11.1M (see Section IV.F) and/or (b) raises the replacement-CEO cost to $0.75M–$1.0M supports Adjusted EBITDA of ~$14.5M–$15.0M.\n157:No PFAS-specific backlog breakout is provided. The Customer/Backlog Report discloses total and segment backlog but not a PFAS breakout, and does not split the $14.0M FY2025E PFAS figure between signed contracts and pipeline. This is the single most important projection question and must be requested immediately. Total contracted backlog converting in FY2025 is only $33.0M (33.5% of FY2025E revenue); adding a 35%–40% win rate on the $31.4M pipeline yields ~$11.0M–$12.6M, covering only ~45%–46% of FY2025E. The remaining ~$53M must be sold in-year — normal for a services business, but it means FY2025E is not heavily pre-sold and the PFAS ramp is largely uncontracted.\n161:FCF is thin and capex is likely understated. The CIM reports FY2024 free cash flow of only $2.0M (operating cash flow $9.2M less capex $7.2M) — roughly 12% conversion of the $17.1M Adjusted EBITDA. This is low and is driven by (a) capex at 8.2% of revenue and (b) a working-capital build (accounts receivable grew $2.1M). Two issues compound this:\n162:Maintenance capex is below the sector floor. The CIM classifies maintenance capex at $3.0M (3.4% of revenue). Sector benchmark for environmental-services companies of this size and fleet intensity is 5%–7% of revenue ($4.4M–$6.1M), confirmed by our portfolio data (GreenWorks ~6.2%, Allied Waste ~5.8%). At GreenWorks, pre-sale maintenance capex was understated at ~3.5% and true post-close maintenance capex ran ~6.2% as deferred maintenance and equipment age surfaced. The CES fleet is ~4.5 years old on average with a >$22M replacement value; maintenance capex should be rising, not flat at $3.0M. If true maintenance capex is ~$5.5M (sector midpoint), the $2.5M annual difference flows directly to lower free cash flow. At the CIM’s own capex total, FCF is only $2.0M; if maintenance capex is understated and total capex is correspondingly higher, FCF could be near zero or negative in a maintenance-normalized year. This is a critical issue for a leveraged transaction: the deal is a growth/roll-up story, not a cash-yield LBO, and debt paydown will be slow.\n163:DSO does not reconcile to the balance sheet. The CIM states DSO of 52 days. Period-end accounts receivable of $16.9M against FY2024 revenue of $87.3M implies ~71 days; using average AR ($14.2M→$16.9M) implies ~65 days. Including the exhibits’ separate “unbilled revenue” of $2.7M pushes the figure toward ~82 days. The stated 52-day figure is not reconcilable to any reasonable revenue base and likely reflects a “normalized” rather than actual calculation. The ~14–19 day understatement implies roughly $3.4M–$4.6M of additional working-capital need not reflected in the headline metrics. NWC of $11.8M (13.5% of revenue) is within the 10%–14% sector range, but the DSO discrepancy suggests the receivables component is aging worse than presented.\n202:Cash. $2.3M (CIM) vs. $4.1M (exhibits) — a $1.8M discrepancy.\n207:The CIM and the financial exhibits tell materially different stories for the same periods. This is unusual and is itself a diligence finding — the sell-side data room does not internally reconcile. Key discrepancies (FY2024 unless noted):\n223:Exhibits’ P&L does not foot to EBITDA\n245:$17.1M\n269:Contracted backlog $42.6M (Remediation $26.8M / 62.9%; Industrial Cleaning $11.4M / 26.8%; Emergency Response $4.4M / 10.3%). Conversion: $18.2M in H1 2025, $14.8M in H2 2025, $9.6M in 2026+ — i.e., ~$33.0M (77.5%) converts in FY2025.\n271:Coverage of FY2025E ($98.5M): backlog $33.0M (33.5%) + pipeline yield $11.0M–$12.6M (11%–13%) = ~45%–46% covered; ~$53M must be sold in-year. The PFAS portion of the FY2025E ramp is largely within this uncovered, in-year bucket — reinforcing the need for a PFAS-specific backlog breakout.\n304:EBITDA quality. $1.2M+ of the $17.1M Adjusted EBITDA is not defensible (arithmetic error + lease + regulatory), and the reported-EBITDA base itself is not corroborated by the detailed financials.\n316:Starting from reported EBITDA of $12.1M and applying defensible adjustments (Section IV.B), buy-side normalized FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA is ~$15.9M. A conservative case (exhibits-implied reported EBITDA ~$11.1M and/or higher replacement-CEO cost) supports ~$14.5M–$15.0M. The CIM’s $17.1M is not supported.\n323:CIM stated — $17.1M\n328:Arithmetic-corrected — $16.8M\n383:Reconciled top-10 customer list (resolve the CIM vs. Report name discrepancy for positions #6–#10) with contract type, expiration, and relationship-since for each.\n427:Adjusted EBITDA arithmetic\n428:$17.1M\n429:$16.8M (sum of stated adjustments)\n430:($2.4M–$3.0M)\n440:($2.4M–$5.0M)\n448:$3.0M (3.4% of rev)\n\n--- ITER1 (failed both) ---\n22:The central conclusion of this analysis is that the sell-side materials are internally inconsistent across the CIM, the Customer/Backlog Report, and the Financial Exhibits, and the headline Adjusted EBITDA of $17.1 million is materially overstated. A reconstructed buy-side Adjusted EBITDA is approximately $14.9 million–$16.1 million (midpoint ~$15.5 million) versus the sell-side $17.1 million — a $1.0 million–$2.2 million reduction driven by (i) an above-market lease add-back that conflicts with the CIM’s own market data, (ii) a regulatory-cost add-back that is partly below the EBITDA line and partly recurring in nature, and (iii) other adjustments of questionable “one-time” character. At a defensible 8.0x–9.0x multiple on buy-side Adjusted EBITDA, the implied enterprise value is approximately $119 million–$140 million, below the sell-side’s stated $136.8 million–$171.0 million range and, notably, above Fund III’s stated $30 million–$100 million platform target.\n24:Recommendation: Submit an IOI at an enterprise value range of approximately $120 million–$135 million (cash-free / debt-free, normalized NWC), equivalent to roughly 8.0x–8.7x buy-side Adjusted EBITDA of ~$15.5 million, with a heavily conditional structure (real estate, PNR renewal, PFAS backlog, and key-person retention as go-forward conditions) and the detailed diligence request list in Section XV. We should not anchor to the sell-side’s $17.1 million Adjusted EBITDA or its “higher end of the range” guidance.\n41:Diversified end markets and geography. Six end-market sectors (no single sector >25.7%) and a four-state footprint, with 90% historical MSA renewal rates and no top-10 customer losses in five years (per CBR).\n187:Emergency Response projections differ between CIM ($13.0M FY25E) and FE ($11.8M)\n192:V. Financial Statement Integrity — Cross-Document Discrepancies\n253:17.1\n256:The discrepancies are not rounding: total assets differ by $3.9 million, cash by $1.8 million, PP&E by $2.6 million, and equity by $1.7 million. The historical balance sheets diverge every year (e.g., CIM FY2021 equity $11.8M vs. FE $3.7M — an $8.1M gap). Notably, net working capital happens to tie at $11.8M in both (because the component differences offset), which may create a false sense of consistency. The cash discrepancy alone changes the net-debt bridge by $1.8M and therefore the equity proceeds to sellers in a cash-free/debt-free deal.\n388:Both tie to the same revenue totals each year, but the segment allocations differ by up to $1.0M. The forward segment projections also differ (e.g., Emergency Response FY2025E: CIM $13.0M vs. FE $11.8M; Remediation FY2026E: CIM $65.0M vs. FE $66.0M), again with totals that tie but allocations that do not. The segment mix — which drives the margin-expansion thesis (remediation/PFAS being higher-margin) — is therefore not reliably stated.\n459:The Meridian discrepancy (2014 vs. 2017) is three years. These dates underpin the “longevity / recurring revenue / high switching costs” thesis; the inconsistencies erode confidence in that narrative.\n468:The sell-side Adjusted EBITDA bridge adds back $4.7M to reported EBITDA of $12.1M to reach $17.1M (19.6% margin). The sector memo correctly flags that 19.6% is at the upper boundary of plausibility for this sector (15–18% adjusted is typical) and that each adjustment requires independent verification. This section evaluates each add-back and reconstructs a buy-side figure.\n477:The CIM adds back $1.9M ($2.4M actual total compensation less $500K assumed replacement CEO). The $2.4M is stated to include “salary, annual bonus, benefits, and personal expenses allocated to the Company.” The nature and quantum of “personal expenses allocated to the Company” are undisclosed and must be itemized in QoE — such expenses, if non-business, should be excluded from the add-back (they are not a legitimate replacement-CEO cost; they are owner perquisites that simply should not have been in the business). The $500K replacement-CEO assumption is plausible for the next phase but should be benchmarked. Pending verification, we treat this add-back at $1.5M–$1.9M (reducing by the unverified personal-expense portion).\n526:17.1\n532:17.1%\n581:The CIM and FE both state maintenance capex of $3.0M in FY2024 (3.4% of revenue), with the FE showing maintenance capex declining as a percentage of revenue from 3.9% (FY2022) to 2.7% (FY2027E). The sector benchmark, confirmed by Thornfield’s GreenWorks (6.2%) and Allied Waste (5.8%) portfolio data, is 5–7% of revenue — implying maintenance capex of $4.4M–$6.1M for CES at $87.3M revenue. CES’s figure is 150–200 bps below the sector floor, and a declining maintenance-capex ratio for a growing, capital-intensive fleet is a classic pre-sale deferral signal. At GreenWorks, sell-side maintenance capex was understated at ~3.5% and actual post-close maintenance capex ran ~6.2%, with deferred maintenance and aging equipment becoming apparent only after a detailed fleet assessment.\n582:If true maintenance capex is ~$5.5M (sector midpoint) rather than $3.0M, the $2.5M annual difference directly reduces free cash flow and the implied LBO return. This is one of the most value-relevant findings in the review.\n584:The CIM (Appendix C) discloses 82+ major units (18 vacuum trucks, 12 hydroblasters, 8 excavators, 6 roll-off trucks, 35+ support vehicles, 3 PFAS units) with an average age of ~4.5 years and a total estimated replacement value exceeding $22M. On a 7–10 year useful life, steady-state fleet replacement alone would imply ~$2.2M–$3.1M per year — yet the FE’s “Fleet Maintenance & Replacement” line is only $1.7M in FY2024 (and the total maintenance capex $3.0M includes facility and safety-compliance components as well). The fleet economics are consistent with understated maintenance capex. A detailed fleet age, condition, and remaining-useful-life assessment is a critical early-diligence deliverable.\n593:Both are well above the stated 52 days and at or above the top of the 45–55 day sector range. To reconcile to 52 days, AR would need to be ~$12.4M, not $16.9M — a ~$4.5M gap. Either the DSO is understated, or AR includes significant unbilled/aged balances not reflected in the DSO calculation. This should be independently calculated in QoE from the AR aging and trailing revenue.\n626:The CIM cites “comparable transactions in the environmental services sector at 8.0x–10.0x Adjusted EBITDA” and, on FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA of $17.1M, implies an EV range of $136.8M–$171.0M, suggesting valuation “at the higher end of the comparable range” given growth, PFAS, and platform characteristics. On a forward basis, FY2025E Adjusted EBITDA of $19.7M implies $157.6M–$197.0M. No specific comparable transactions (names, dates, sizes) are provided to support the 8.0x–10.0x range — a gap that should be filled with Thornfield’s internal database and independent comps.\n628:Anchoring on the buy-side Adjusted EBITDA of ~$15.5M (Section VI.G) rather than the sell-side $17.1M, and given the adjustment-quality, PFAS-execution, capex, and customer-contract risks (all of which argue for the lower end of the range, not the higher end the sell-side advocates):\n635:17.1\n671:Owner compensation detail. Itemization of the $2.4M total compensation, specifically the “personal expenses allocated to the Company”; replacement-CEO benchmarking. (Section VI.C)\n673:Customer list and contracts. Verified top-25 customer list with executed contracts (to resolve the CIM/CBR customer-name discrepancy); renewal history; concentration trends. (Section V.G, VIII)\n686:Indication of value: EV range ~$120M–$135M, cash-free/debt-free with normalized NWC, stated as ~8.0x–8.7x buy-side Adjusted EBITDA of ~$15.5M (FY2024A). Explicitly note that the figure is based on a normalized EBITDA that adjusts the sell-side bridge (lease, regulatory, relocation) and is subject to QoE. Decline to anchor on the sell-side $17.1M.\n702:This memo should be read together with Sarah Lindgren’s June 12 sector memo, whose benchmarks and portfolio lessons (GreenWorks, Allied Waste) are incorporated throughout.
