{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01B8pkWsrHHVmCz6yQaWgRtd","type":"tool_result","content":"===================== C-001: does iter1 EVER mention the 16.8 foot error? =====================\n--- ITER1 grep for 16.8 / 4.7 sum / foot / arithmetic ---\n41:Diversified end markets and geography. Six end-market sectors (no single sector >25.7%) and a four-state footprint, with 90% historical MSA renewal rates and no top-10 customer losses in five years (per CBR).\n\nRESULT: (empty above = iter1 dropped the arithmetic error entirely)\n\n===================== C-003: replacement CEO too low =====================\n--- ITER0 (PASSED C-003) lines with 0.5M / replacement CEO ---\n25:The related-party lease add-back is contradicted by the CIM’s own market data. The $0.8M add-back assumes “market rent” of $0.6M against the $1.4M paid to the founder/owner. But the CIM’s own Industry Overview states that comparable specialized environmental staging facilities command $1.0M–$1.2M annually. On the CIM’s own data, the defensible add-back is $0.2M–$0.4M, not $0.8M — an overstatement of $0.4M–$0.6M ($3.2M–$6.0M of EV).\n26:The regulatory/legal add-back treats a recurring cost as one-time. The full $1.2M DEQ enforcement add-back is inconsistent with sector benchmarks ($0.2M–$0.5M annual recurring regulatory run-rate) and with Thornfield’s own portfolio experience at GreenWorks and Allied Waste. A defensible add-back is $0.7M–$0.9M.\n102:(2) The related-party lease add-back is contradicted by the CIM’s own market data. The $0.8M add-back assumes market rent of $0.6M against the $1.4M paid to Mr. Oakes (who owns the HQ personally). However, the CIM’s Industry Overview states that “specialized environmental staging facilities commanding rates of $1.0 million to $1.2 million annually for properties of comparable acreage and infrastructure.” The CES HQ is precisely such a facility (8.5 acres, permitted wash rack, secondary containment, roll-off/soil-stockpile staging). On the CIM’s own comparable, market rent is $1.0M–$1.2M, making the defensible above-market add-back $0.2M–$0.4M, not $0.8M. The overstatement is $0.4M–$0.6M ($3.2M–$6.0M of EV). This also has a real-estate/hold-up dimension addressed in Section VI.\n103:(3) The regulatory/legal add-back treats a recurring cost as one-time. The $1.2M add-back ($875K DEQ settlement + $325K legal fees, August 14, 2024) is presented as fully non-recurring. Sector benchmarks and Thornfield’s portfolio experience say otherwise: environmental-services companies of this size incur $0.2M–$0.5M per year of recurring regulatory/compliance cost (routine defense, permit renewals, audits, minor enforcement responses, fines). At GreenWorks, a “$380K one-time” add-back was followed by ~$275K/year of recurring regulatory expense; at Allied Waste, recurring regulatory costs averaged ~$310K/year with a spike to $840K. Best-practice QoE normalizes to a run-rate and adds back only the excess. Assuming a $0.3M–$0.5M run-rate, the defensible add-back is $0.7M–$0.9M, an overstatement of $0.3M–$0.5M ($2.4M–$5.0M of EV). This requires CES’s full 10-year enforcement history to confirm the DEQ matter is isolated rather than systemic — particularly because it involved improper storage of contaminated soils at the Portland facility, an operational compliance failure at the core site.\n105:Owner compensation normalization ($1.9M). $2.4M total comp (including “personal expenses allocated to the Company”) less a $0.5M replacement-CEO assumption. The personal-expense component must be substantiated as truly personal. The $0.5M replacement assumption may be light: a PE-licensed environmental-services CEO in the Pacific Northwest market could plausibly cost $0.75M–$1.0M total, which would reduce the defensible add-back to $1.4M–$1.65M.\n153:The buy-side figure of ~$15.9M is ~7% below the CIM’s $17.1M. A conservative case that also (a) uses the exhibits-implied reported EBITDA of ~$11.1M (see Section IV.F) and/or (b) raises the replacement-CEO cost to $0.75M–$1.0M supports Adjusted EBITDA of ~$14.5M–$15.0M.\n251:$0.8M gap\n316:Starting from reported EBITDA of $12.1M and applying defensible adjustments (Section IV.B), buy-side normalized FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA is ~$15.9M. A conservative case (exhibits-implied reported EBITDA ~$11.1M and/or higher replacement-CEO cost) supports ~$14.5M–$15.0M. The CIM’s $17.1M is not supported.\n387:Adjusted EBITDA bridge rebuilt from audited financials; substantiation of every add-back, including personal expenses in owner compensation and the replacement-CEO assumption.\n433:$0.8M (market rent $0.6M)\n\n--- ITER1 (FAILED C-003) lines with 0.5M / replacement CEO ---\n38:Reported EBITDA margin within sector range. Reported FY2024 EBITDA margin of 13.9% ($12.1 million) sits comfortably within the 10–15% sector benchmark for lower-middle-market environmental services companies — indicating a reasonably well-managed operation before the (questionable) adjustments.\n257:Debt presentation is also inconsistent. The CIM states total term-loan principal of $14.2M, split $2.3M current / $11.9M non-current. The FE shows a $1.8M “current portion of long-term debt” and a separate $14.2M “Senior Term Loan” in long-term liabilities — implying either a $16.0M total term loan (if additive) or a double-count. The current portion differs by $0.5M and the non-current portion by $2.3M. This must be reconciled because it drives the net-debt assumption in the EV-to-equity bridge.\n474:Recurring nature: The sector memo establishes, with direct GreenWorks and Allied Waste portfolio evidence, that regulatory and compliance costs in this sector are recurring at approximately $200K–$500K per year for companies of CES’s size. Oregon DEQ enforcement has been “particularly active.” It is inappropriate to treat the full amount as one-time; QoE best practice is to normalize to an ongoing run-rate and add back only the excess. Request CES’s complete 10-year regulatory enforcement history to determine whether the 2024 matter is isolated or systemic.\n475:On a run-rate of $300K–$500K, and recognizing that only ~$325K of the $1.2M is even in EBITDA, the defensible regulatory add-back is likely $0K–$325K (and could be zero if the in-EBITDA legal fees are deemed part of the recurring run-rate). This is a reduction of $875K–$1.2M from the sell-side add-back.\n477:The CIM adds back $1.9M ($2.4M actual total compensation less $500K assumed replacement CEO). The $2.4M is stated to include “salary, annual bonus, benefits, and personal expenses allocated to the Company.” The nature and quantum of “personal expenses allocated to the Company” are undisclosed and must be itemized in QoE — such expenses, if non-business, should be excluded from the add-back (they are not a legitimate replacement-CEO cost; they are owner perquisites that simply should not have been in the business). The $500K replacement-CEO assumption is plausible for the next phase but should be benchmarked. Pending verification, we treat this add-back at $1.5M–$1.9M (reducing by the unverified personal-expense portion).\n581:The CIM and FE both state maintenance capex of $3.0M in FY2024 (3.4% of revenue), with the FE showing maintenance capex declining as a percentage of revenue from 3.9% (FY2022) to 2.7% (FY2027E). The sector benchmark, confirmed by Thornfield’s GreenWorks (6.2%) and Allied Waste (5.8%) portfolio data, is 5–7% of revenue — implying maintenance capex of $4.4M–$6.1M for CES at $87.3M revenue. CES’s figure is 150–200 bps below the sector floor, and a declining maintenance-capex ratio for a growing, capital-intensive fleet is a classic pre-sale deferral signal. At GreenWorks, sell-side maintenance capex was understated at ~3.5% and actual post-close maintenance capex ran ~6.2%, with deferred maintenance and aging equipment becoming apparent only after a detailed fleet assessment.\n603:As developed in Section VI.B, regulatory and compliance costs in this sector are inherently recurring ($200K–$500K/year per sector benchmarks and Thornfield portfolio data). The full $1.2M add-back is not defensible as one-time. A normalized ongoing compliance run-rate should be built into the operating model, with only true excess amounts added back.\n616:Mr. Oakes (age 61, 68% equity, CEO and Chairman) “oversees all strategic, financial, and operational decisions” (CIM §VII.B) and intends to transition out of day-to-day operations within 18–24 months post-close, remaining in an advisory capacity. The Company is highly founder-led with concentrated decision-making. The owner-compensation add-back assumes a $500K replacement CEO; the buyer must have a credible succession plan and be prepared to recruit (and pay for) a CEO and potentially strengthen the CFO/Controller function (currently a Controller, not a CFO). The process letter asks bidders to address willingness to accommodate the transition period; we should propose a structured transition with retention/consulting arrangements and clear milestones.\n671:Owner compensation detail. Itemization of the $2.4M total compensation, specifically the “personal expenses allocated to the Company”; replacement-CEO benchmarking. (Section VI.C)\n690:Management/operations: Retain existing management and workforce; accommodate Mr. Oakes’s 18–24 month transition in an advisory/consulting role; recruit a replacement CEO and additional PEs; implement retention agreements for Ms. Prewitt, Mr. Cahill, and PEs as a closing condition.","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_0156rfzvU6zUanWCLDMqZ2qf","session_id":"ad004501-7372-4a84-90be-02a36df6419c","uuid":"255b1e82-ba49-4ed6-a7f2-db4327ef1383","timestamp":"2026-06-27T21:40:55.048Z","subagent_type":"general-purpose","task_description":"Analyze cim-teaser regression"}

Stonemill’s board observer right — does it survive?\n215:Section 4.1 grants Stonemill the right to appoint a non-voting board observer for so long as Stonemill holds at least 1,000,000 shares of Series C (or Common issued upon conversion). The observer is entitled to attend all Board and committee meetings and to receive all materials provided to directors (subject to attorney-client privilege and conflict-of-interest exclusions under Section 4.2).\n216:The observer right does not survive the merger. Two independent reasons: (1) the Voting Agreement terminates at closing under Section 7.1(b) (Sale of the Company); and (2) even if the Voting Agreement did not terminate, the observer right is conditioned on Stonemill holding ≥1,000,000 Series C (or conversion Common), and at closing Stonemill’s Series C is cancelled for cash — so the condition fails and the right expires under Section 7.3. In that scenario, the designation rights and voting obligations would technically remain in force. However, the practical impact is limited: post-closing, the former stockholders (Founders and Investors) no longer hold shares (cashed out), so their voting obligations are moot, and the individual designation/observer rights expire under Section 7.3 when the minimum-share thresholds are no longer met. The residual risk is low but non-zero, and it reinforces the importance of the definitional-consistency analysis in Section VIII. We recommend that the merger agreement include an express termination/waiver of the Voting Agreement at closing to eliminate any sunset-related lingering risk.\n295:The Stonemill board observer was also absent from the April 17, 2024 meeting. If the observer was not given proper notice or was not provided the meeting materials, Stonemill could claim a breach of the observer right, reinforcing its narrative that it was excluded from the sale process at a critical juncture. Given Stonemill’s adversarial posture, the Company should be prepared to invoke this exclusion judiciously for privileged deliberations and conflict-sensitive matters. Equity Plan Share Reserve Increase (July 2023) — Stockholder Approval Question\n231\tAt the July 19, 2023 Board meeting, the Board approved an amendment to the Plan increasing the share reserve from 8,000,000 to 9,500,000 shares (a 1,500,000-share increase). The minutes reflect that outside counsel (Mr. Rourke) “noted that the amendment required Board approval,” and the Board approved the increase 7-0. The minutes do not reflect any stockholder approval.\n232\tWhether stockholder approval was required depends on the Plan’s terms (which we have not received). Under Delaware law, increasing a plan’s share reserve does not increase the authorized capital stock (and thus does not trigger the Charter’s Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) protective provision, which concerns authorized shares). However, stockholder approval may be required (i) if the Plan so provides, (ii) to maintain incentive stock option (“ISO”) qualification under Section 422 of the Internal Revenue Code (if the Plan grants ISOs), or (iii) for other tax or securities-law reasons. As a private company, exchange-listing standards (e.g., the 20% rule) do not apply. We must review the Plan to determine whether stockholder approval was required and, if so, whether it was obtained. Severity: Moderate (pending Plan review).\n233\tThe anti-dilution provisions of the Charter (Section 4.3.4(b)(i)) exempt shares issued “pursuant to equity incentive plans or stock purchase plans approved by the Board of Directors, including at least one director elected by the holders of Preferred Stock.” The Board includes Preferred-designated directors (Messrs. Kleinhans and Trask and Ms. Okafor), so grants under the Plan are exempt from anti-dilution adjustment. No anti-dilution issue is presented by the reserve increase or the grants.\n234\t\n235\tX. AREA 8 — AUTHORIZATION OF CORPORATE ACTIONS: BOARD MINUTES REVIEW\n236\tWe reviewed all eight sets of Board minutes (January 18, 2023 through September 25, 2024). For each significant corporate action, we assessed whether proper Board approval was obtained and whether stockholder or Preferred Stockholder consent was required and, if so, whether it was obtained.\n237\tA. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01Th5fDu9Qh2HhPMmNhFJ4oV","type":"tool_result","content":"############ C-038 WRITTEN CONSENT mechanism ############\n--- iter0 ---\n12:The central finding of this diligence is that Stonemill Capital Partners Fund VI, LP (“Stonemill”) holds a hard, charter-level blocking right over the Transaction. As the holder of 100% of the outstanding Series C Preferred Stock, Stonemill alone controls the Series C separate-class protective provision under Section 4.3.6(b)(i) of the Restated Certificate, which requires the affirmative vote or written consent of holders of at least 66⅔% of the Series C Preferred Stock, voting as a separate class, to effect any “Liquidation Event.” The reverse triangular merger is unambiguously a “Liquidation Event.” Because Stonemill holds all 7,800,000 outstanding Series C shares, the 66⅔% threshold (≥5,200,000 shares) cannot be satisfied without Stonemill’s affirmative consent. This blocking right cannot be cured by the drag-along mechanism in the Stockholders’ Agreement with any certainty under Delaware law, as discussed in Section IV.\n103:DGCL § 251(c) requires the approval of holders of a majority of the outstanding shares of stock entitled to vote (the Company’s Restated Certificate and the protective provisions cause the Preferred to vote on an as-converted basis with the Common as a single class on this matter). This is a majority of the approximately 33,900,000 fully diluted shares, i.e., more than 16,950,000 shares. Status: Not yet obtained. This vote can be taken by written consent in lieu of a meeting under DGCL § 228 and Restated Certificate Article VI, Section 6.2 / Bylaws Section 2.6.\n105:So long as any Preferred Stock is outstanding, the Company may not “effect any Liquidation Event” without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of at least a majority (greater than 50%) of the then-outstanding Preferred Stock, voting together as a single class on an as-converted basis (Section 4.3.6(a)(vi)). The Restated Certificate specifies that this requires the affirmative vote or consent of holders of more than 8,250,000 shares of Preferred (based on 16,500,000 Preferred shares outstanding: 3,500,000 Series A; 5,200,000 Series B; 7,800,000 Series C).\n108:So long as any Series C Preferred Stock is outstanding, the Company may not “effect any Liquidation Event” without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of at least 66⅔% of the then-outstanding Series C Preferred Stock, voting as a separate class (Section 4.3.6(b)(i)). This consent is “separate from and in addition to” the all-Preferred consent under Section 4.3.6(a). The Restated Certificate specifies that this requires the affirmative vote or consent of holders of at least 5,200,000 shares of Series C (based on 7,800,000 Series C shares outstanding).\n113:We considered whether the merger could be structured to avoid a stockholder vote under DGCL § 251(f) (no stockholder vote required where, among other things, no amendment to the certificate of incorporation is made in the merger and either the acquirer owns 90% of the target or certain other conditions are met). This is not available here: Hargrove does not currently own any Terravox stock, and the merger will cancel all outstanding equity for consideration. A stockholder vote (or written consent) is required.\n147:This is the decisive question for the Transaction. Even if the drag-along is triggered without Stonemill and compels Stonemill to vote its Series C in favor, the question is whether that compelled vote satisfies the charter’s separate-class protective provision in Section 4.3.6(b)(i), which requires the “affirmative vote or written consent” of 66⅔% of the Series C voting as a separate class.\n149:The charter is satisfied by a vote, not by a discretionary veto. Section 4.3.6(b) is satisfied by “the prior written consent or affirmative vote” of 66⅔% of the Series C. It does not require a voluntary or uncoerced consent; it requires a vote or consent of the requisite threshold. If the drag-along compels Stonemill (100% of the Series C) to vote in favor, that is an “affirmative vote” of 100% of the Series C, which exceeds the 66⅔% threshold. On this reading, the charter requirement is satisfied, not waived or amended — the protective provision is given effect through a compelled vote.\n212:Section 7.1 provides that the Voting Agreement terminates upon the earliest of: (a) an IPO; (b) the consummation of a Sale of the Company (as defined in Section 1); (c) written consent of the Company, a majority of the Founder Common, and a majority of each series of Preferred held by parties (voting as separate classes); or (d) November 3, 2026 (the fifth anniversary — the “sunset”).\n266:Recommendation. Request the stockholder consent records (written consents) for the period around October 2023 to confirm whether Preferred consent was obtained. If not, assess cure options (ratification) and disclose in the merger agreement reps.\n308:Registration rights terminate with respect to a particular Holder upon the earliest of: (a) the Holder being able to sell all its Registrable Securities in a single three-month period under Rule 144 without volume/manner-of-sale restrictions and without the current-public-information requirement; (b) the fifth anniversary of the IPO closing; or (c) “a Deemed Liquidation Event.” The Agreement terminates in its entirety upon the earliest of (i) termination as to all Holders under (a)/(b)/(c), or (ii) the written consent of the Company and Holders of a majority of the then-outstanding Registrable Securities.\n--- iter1 ---\n71:1. All-Preferred protective provision (Section 4.3.6(a)(vi)). So long as any Preferred Stock is outstanding, the Company may not “effect any Liquidation Event” without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of at least a majority (greater than 50%) of the then-outstanding Preferred Stock, voting together as a single class on an as-converted basis. The Charter specifies that this requires the affirmative vote or consent of holders of more than 8,250,000 shares of Preferred Stock (based on 16,500,000 shares outstanding).\n74:2. Series C separate-class protective provision (Section 4.3.6(b)(i)). So long as any Series C Preferred Stock is outstanding, the Company may not “effect any Liquidation Event” without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of at least 66⅔% of the then-outstanding Series C Preferred Stock, voting as a separate class. The Charter specifies that this requires the affirmative vote or consent of holders of at least 5,200,000 shares of Series C (based on 7,800,000 shares outstanding).\n145:Section 7.1 of the Voting Agreement provides that the agreement terminates upon the earliest of: (a) an IPO; (b) “the consummation of a Sale of the Company (as defined in Section 1)”; (c) written consent of the Company and specified stockholder majorities; or (d) November 3, 2026 (the fifth anniversary).\n254:Recommendation. Request the Clearfield loan agreement and ancillary documents to assess change-of-control covenants. If the consent was not obtained, assess remediation options (e.g., ratification by the Preferred holders) and evaluate the risk that Stonemill raises this issue. Severity: Significant.\n288:Section 9 of the RRA provides that the registration rights granted thereunder terminate with respect to any particular Holder upon the earliest of: (a) the date the Holder can sell all of its Registrable Securities in a single three-month period under Rule 144 without volume or manner-of-sale restrictions and without the current-public-information requirement; (b) the fifth anniversary of the closing of the Company’s IPO; or (c) “a Deemed Liquidation Event.” The RRA terminates in its entirety upon the earliest of (i) termination as to all Holders pursuant to clauses (a)–(c), or (ii) the written consent of the Company and Holders of a majority of the then-outstanding Registrable Securities.\n368:Stockholder consent records — specifically: (a) any Preferred Stockholder written consent in connection with the $12 million Clearfield term loan (October 2023); (b) any stockholder consent in connection with the Plan share-reserve increase (July 2023); and (c) all other stockholder written consents since November 3, 2021.\n\n############ Section / 'Area' headers in each (structure) ############\n--- iter0 roman-numeral / lettered section headers ---\n10:I. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01BPH7U8b4AHZbVwdBR1nWEn","type":"tool_result","content":"############ C-007 per-series liq pref figures in iter1 (passed) ############\n200:<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">$144,300,000 ($18.50/sh)\n202:<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">$18.50\n203:<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">$144,300,000\n206:<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">$48,100,000 ($9.25/sh)\n212:<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">$14,000,000 ($4.00/sh)\n222:Methodology: Series C takes its $18.50/share liquidation preference ($144.3 million) because converting would yield only approximately $13.95/share on a pro rata basis across all 33,900,000 fully diluted shares — less than the preference. The remaining approximately $328.7 million is shared pro rata among Series B, Series A, and Common (26,100,000 shares), yielding approximately $12.59/share. Series B and Series A each convert because $12.59/share exceeds their respective preferences ($9.25 and $4.00).\n224:The September 25, 2024 Board minutes’ characterization appears imprecise. The minutes state that “all holders of Preferred Stock would receive amounts in excess of their respective liquidation preferences on an as-converted basis, suggesting that participation on an as-converted basis would be economically favorable for all preferred holders.” In fact, Series C does not receive an amount in excess of its liquidation preference — it receives exactly its preference ($18.50/share) and does not participate on an as-converted basis (because as-converted participation would yield less). Only Series B and Series A receive amounts in excess of their preferences. This imprecision in the Board’s characterization should be noted; it may reflect an oversimplification in the minutes or in Meridian’s preliminary analysis, and it is relevant to the fairness analysis (Section X, Area 9).\n225:Stonemill’s economic position explains its resistance. At $485 million enterprise value, Stonemill receives approximately $144.3 million — essentially a return of its Original Issue Price ($18.50 × 7,800,000), with no gain. Meanwhile, Northbridge, Ridgeline, and the Common holders capture the upside above the preferences. This is the economic root of Stonemill’s opposition: the Series C liquidation preference is so large relative to the equity value that Stonemill is, in effect, the “last money in” bearing the risk with no upside at this price. Stonemill’s likely objective — a higher allocation of consideration or a side arrangement — is economically rational from its perspective and should inform the negotiation strategy.\n\n############ iter1: does it tie the 200k RSU / 350k options to the increased pool / validity? ############\n14:The all-Preferred protective provision CAN be satisfied without Stonemill. Section 4.3.6(a)(vi) requires the consent of holders of a majority of the Preferred Stock (voting as a single class on an as-converted basis), a threshold of more than 8,250,000 of the 16,500,000 outstanding Preferred shares. Northbridge (5,200,000) and Ridgeline (3,500,000) together hold 8,700,000 shares, sufficient to satisfy this threshold without Stonemill. This narrows Stonemill’s blocking position to the Series C separate-class vote alone.\n38:<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">4,200,000\n50:<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">5,200,000\n73:This threshold CAN be satisfied without Stonemill. Northbridge (5,200,000 shares) and Ridgeline (3,500,000 shares) together hold 8,700,000 shares, which exceeds the 8,250,000-share threshold. Stonemill’s 7,800,000 shares are not required.\n74:2. Series C separate-class protective provision (Section 4.3.6(b)(i)). So long as any Series C Preferred Stock is outstanding, the Company may not “effect any Liquidation Event” without the prior written consent or affirmative vote of holders of at least 66⅔% of the then-outstanding Series C Preferred Stock, voting as a separate class. The Charter specifies that this requires the affirmative vote or consent of holders of at least 5,200,000 shares of Series C (based on 7,800,000 shares outstanding).\n97:Condition (ii) — Preferred vote: The threshold is a majority of the 16,500,000 outstanding Preferred shares (as-converted), i.e., more than 8,250,000 shares. Without Stonemill, Northbridge (5,200,000) and Ridgeline (3,500,000) together hold 8,700,000 shares — sufficient to satisfy this condition. Satisfiable without Stonemill.\n99:Condition (i) — Common vote: This condition is the principal uncertainty. The provision requires a majority of the outstanding Common Stock “calculated on an as-converted to Common Stock basis, treating each share of Preferred Stock as having been converted.” Read naturally, this is a majority of the fully diluted as-converted base. On a fully diluted basis of approximately 33,900,000 shares, a majority requires more than approximately 16,950,000 shares. The non-Stonemill parties to the Stockholders’ Agreement (the Founders, Ridgeline, and Northbridge) collectively hold 16,000,000 as-converted shares (Founders 7,300,000 + Ridgeline 3,500,000 + Northbridge 5,200,000) — approximately 950,000 shares short of the threshold. Satisfaction of this condition without Stonemill would therefore require the support of additional Common holders who are not parties to the Stockholders’ Agreement (a portion of the 10,100,000 shares held by “Various Common Holders / Option Pool”). The availability of that support cannot be confirmed without the complete capitalization table and clarification of whether unexercised options and unvested RSUs are counted as “outstanding shares of Common Stock.” Uncertain — likely requires non-party Common holder support.\n187:Raj Malhotra (VP of Engineering): 350,000 stock options, exercise price $14.30 per share, four-year vesting with a one-year cliff, granted April 12, 2023 under the Plan.\n188:Linda Yeung (CFO): 200,000 RSUs, three-year vesting (one-third per year), granted April 17, 2024 under the Plan (as amended).\n205:<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">5,200,000\n227:Malhotra options (350,000 @ $14.30): The implied Common per-share consideration (~$12.59) is below the $14.30 exercise price, meaning these options are currently underwater. If the options are cashed out at closing on a single-trigger basis, the net value would be zero (or the options would be cancelled for no consideration). However, this depends on the final per-share consideration (which depends on the final equity value and waterfall) and the Plan’s treatment of underwater options. If the deal value increases, the options could move into the money.\n228:Yeung RSUs (200,000): RSUs have no exercise price. If single-trigger accelerated, 200,000 RSUs × ~$12.59/share ≈ $2.5 million. As of a Q1 2025 closing, only approximately one-third would be vested absent acceleration.\n239:Meeting 2 — April 12, 2023 (7/7 present; quorum satisfied). Received Meridian’s strategic-alternatives presentation; formed the Special Committee (Lau, Garrett, Okafor); and approved the Malhotra option grant (350,000 @ $14.30). All approved 7-0. The Special Committee composition is analyzed in Section XI (Area 9). The option grant is exempt from anti-dilution under Charter Section 4.3.4(b)(i). Properly authorized at the Board level.\n243:Meeting 6 — April 17, 2024 (5/7 present; quorum satisfied (4 required)). Trask and Garrett absent (Trask: scheduling conflict, no proxy; Garrett: family medical matter). Authorized the Special Committee to engage with Hargrove and provide data-room access (approved 5-0); approved the Yeung RSU grant (200,000). Trask objected by email on April 19, 2024 (appended to the minutes). The Stonemill observer was not present. Analyzed in Sections X.C and XI (Area 9).\n266:Yeung RSU grant. The 200,000 RSU grant to Linda Yeung (CFO and recording secretary) was approved by the Board (5-0) at the April 17, 2024 meeting. Ms. Yeung serves as both CFO and recording secretary; her receipt of equity awards approved by the Board she documents is a minor governance optics point but is not unusual. The grant is exempt from anti-dilution under Charter Section 4.3.4(b)(i).\n366:All individual award agreements under the Plan — in particular the option agreement for Raj Malhotra (350,000 options) and the RSU agreement for Linda Yeung (200,000 RSUs), and all other outstanding option and RSU agreements.\n420:<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:rPr><w:b /><w:bCs /></w:rPr><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">Not yet obtained. Can be satisfied without Stonemill (Northbridge 5,200,000 + Ridgeline 3,500,000 = 8,700,000).\n424:<w:tcPr /><w:p><w:pPr><w:pStyle w:val=\"Compact\" /><w:jc w:val=\"left\" /></w:pPr><w:r><w:t xml:space=\"preserve\">66⅔% of Series C, separate class (≥5,200,000 of 7,800,000 shares)\n448:All individual award agreements (options, RSUs) — especially Malhotra and Yeung, and all others.\n\n############ iter0: equity-plan section header context (lines 250-255) ############\nD. Share-reserve increase — stockholder approval (Significant)\nThe Plan share reserve was increased from 8,000,000 to 9,500,000 shares on July 19, 2023, by Board resolution only (approved 7-0). Two questions arise:\nPlan-required stockholder approval. Many VC-backed equity incentive plans require stockholder approval for amendments that increase the share reserve. We cannot confirm whether the Plan requires stockholder approval without reviewing the Plan. If the Plan requires stockholder approval and it was not obtained, the amendment may be invalid and awards granted under the increased reserve may be vulnerable.\nCharter protective provisions. Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) requires Preferred consent to “increase or decrease the total number of authorized shares of Common Stock or Preferred Stock, or any series thereof.” The share-reserve increase does not increase the authorized Common Stock (50,000,000 authorized, unchanged); it merely reserves additional authorized shares for issuance under the Plan. Accordingly, Section 4.3.6(a)(ii) is likely not triggered. However, the anti-dilution exception in Section 4.3.4(b)(i) exempts options issued under a Board-approved plan (including at least one Preferred-designated director) from anti-dilution adjustment, so the increase should not trigger Preferred anti-dilution. We nonetheless recommend confirming that no other protective provision (e.g., a plan-specific consent right in a separate Investors’ Rights Agreement, if one exists — see Section VIII.C) was implicated.\nRecommendation. Request the Plan (as amended), the amendment effecting the share-reserve increase, and any stockholder consent records relating to the increase. Confirm whether stockholder approval was required and obtained; if not, assess the validity of the increase and consider a ratification/curative consent.","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01KauSjt43sXTapGyaSEvhoj","session_id":"ad004501-7372-4a84-90be-02a36df6419c","uuid":"4c13aa22-a518-4538-acc2-34c95457a58b","timestamp":"2026-06-27T21:41:23.508Z","subagent_type":"general-purpose","task_description":"Analyze review-governance-diligence regression"}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01HbHM3kXKvrAenAgJSFxYgY","type":"tool_result","content":"=== run_harvey_agent.md ===\n# Tool — `run_harvey_agent`\n\n> The mutator invokes this from its shell to run a BATCH of Harvey rollouts concurrently, for triage.\n\n## Purpose\n\nRun the **frozen Harvey agent** on **one or many task ids** (a **batch**, run **concurrently**),\nusing your **current `harness/`**: the broker overlays your edited `system_prompt.md` / `tools.py` /\n`agent_loop.py` onto the bare harvey-labs checkout and runs that (exactly what the standard eval\nwould run), producing a deliverable + trajectory **per task** you can inspect and proxy-grade. This\nis how you *try* a harness edit before the canonical eval sees it — and how you buy **breadth**:\ntest one edit across several tasks in a **single call** (they run in parallel) instead of one slow\nserial rollout at a time.\n\nThe Harvey agent is **frozen** here: this tool does not change the agent, the model, or the\nendpoint — it executes the harness as-is. Your edits to `harness/` are what change the output.\n\n## Interface\n\n- **Input:** one or more `task_id`s from your **TRAIN access set** (the matters under `rollouts/` /\n  the train manifest), space-separated: `run_harvey_agent <task_id> [task_id2 ...]`. The batch runs\n  concurrently (capped at a few at a time). Held-out **val** ids are **not available**; a batch\n  containing **any** non-train id is **refused whole**.\n- **Output (all under `scratch/`):** one dir per task — `scratch/runs/<task_id>/deliverable.md`\n  (printed as `DELIVERABLE_PATH=` for `proxy_eval`) **and `scratch/runs/<task_id>/turns.txt`** (the\n  rollout's turn count, also printed as `turns=`) for **`check_runaway`** (the runaway gate). Tasks\n  past the remaining budget come back as `skipped_for_budget` (rerun after you free budget); a task\n  that errors is reported but doesn't sink the rest of the batch.\n- Writes **nothing** outside `scratch/`.\n\n## Cost\n\n≈ **~$1 per task** (one Harvey-agent rollout) × the number of tasks in the batch. **Metered** — see\n`budget.md`; a batch is charged per task only up to the remaining cap (the excess is skipped, not\ncharged). The unit cost is surfaced by the `budget` tool so you can size your batch.\n\n## Guardrails\n\n- **Counts against the budget.** Charged per task, pre-spend. If the **whole** batch is over budget\n  the call returns OVER_BUDGET (your \"commit now\" cue); a partially-over batch runs what fits and\n  returns the rest as `skipped_for_budget`. Check `budget` to size your batch. The budget is there to\n  be **used**: confirm a candidate across **several distinct train tasks in one batch** (and against\n  bare on the same tasks), not a single rollout — breadth across different matters is what shows the\n  edit *generalizes* to tasks you can't see, and it's the main thing to spend the budget on\n  (CLAUDE.md §3). A near-empty ledger means you under-experimented.\n- **Never writes to `rollouts/`.** `rollouts/` is the canonical standard-eval path, owned by the\n  outer driver. This tool is private triage; its outputs are ephemeral `scratch/` artifacts and\n  **must not feed selection**.\n- **Train-only — the val set is unreachable.** Only TRAIN task ids resolve; the held-out val\n  tasks are never mounted into the workspace and any non-train id is refused. Running the agent on\n  a val task would contaminate the held-out verdict, so it is impossible by construction.\n- Frozen agent: do not use this to \"grade your own homework\" — pair it with `proxy_eval` for a\n  cheap private signal only, never as the verdict.\n\n=== proxy_eval.md ===\n# Tool spec — `proxy_eval`\n\n> The mutator invokes this from its shell to triage a deliverable it produced with `run_harvey_agent`.\n\n## Purpose\n\nGrade **ONE deliverable** with a **cheap proxy grader** — a **single mid-tier judge**, NOT the\nfull cascaded ensemble — to get a fast, **private triage** signal on whether a harness edit\nhelped. Use it to rank your own attempts mid-rollout; that is its only job.\n\n## Interface\n\n- **Input:** the **deliverable** (a path under `scratch/`, or the text), plus the **rubric /\n  criteria** for that task (the same criteria shape found in each `rollouts/<task>/task.md`).\n- **Output (under `scratch/`):** a **score** (e.g. pass-rate + per-criterion verdicts) written\n  to `scratch/`, e.g. alongside the run dir. Returns the score so you can compare attempts.\n\n## Cost\n\n**Cheaper than the standard ensemble** — one mid-tier judge call per deliverable, no cascade,\nno strong-model escalation. **Metered** — see `budget.md`; the unit cost is surfaced by `budget`.\n\n## The critical wall — triage only, NEVER the verdict\n\nThis is the load-bearing guard (\"no grading your own homework\"):\n\n- **Private triage only.** The proxy never feeds selection. A proxy calibrated on one harness\n  mis-ranks the moment the harness changes — and could be gamed — so it can only *hint*.\n- **Must NEVER read or run the canonical standard eval**, and must never reproduce it. The\n  standard eval is run by the **outer driver**, not by any tool you can call.\n- **Writes only under `scratch/`. Never writes to `rollouts/`.** No proxy artifact may reach the\n  canonical path.\n- **Train deliverables only.** You only ever triage attempts you produced on your TRAIN tasks\n  (via `run_harvey_agent`). The held-out val set is invisible to you, so it can never be proxy-graded.\n- **NO strong-model APIs** (no GPT-5 / Claude / Opus). A single mid-tier judge, full stop. If a\n  proxy config blows the budget, write a `.claude/memory/` lesson so the run gets cheaper.\n\n=== check_runaway.md ===\n# Tool — `check_runaway`\n\n> The mutator invokes this after a structural edit to catch a turn-count blowup BEFORE committing.\n\n## Purpose\n\nA **hard gate** on the single failure that wastes a whole iteration: a `agent_loop.py` / `tools.py`\nedit that makes the agent **run away** (take many more turns than the bare harness) — usually from an\nunbounded or stacked enforced step. The eval's **canary aborts the entire iteration** when an edit's\nturns exceed **3× the bare baseline**, so check that *yourself* first and reject a runaway edit before\nyou ship it.\n\n## Interface\n\n- **Input:** the task ids you just tested with `run_harvey_agent` — `check_runaway <task_id> [task_id2 ...]`.\n- **What it reads (no cost, local files only):**\n  - your edit's turns — `scratch/runs/<task>/turns.txt` (written by `run_harvey_agent`),\n  - the bare baseline turns — `rollouts/<task>/iter0/metrics.json` (`turn_count`).\n- **Output:** per task, `<edit> turns vs bare <n> = <r>x -> ok | RUNAWAY`. **Exits non-zero (FAIL)\n  if ANY task exceeds 3× bare** — that exit code is your automatic \"this edit is rejected\" signal.\n\n## How to use it (hard rule — see CLAUDE.md §2/§8)\n\nAfter **any** `agent_loop.py` / `tools.py` edit:\n1. `run_harvey_agent <a few tasks>` (a batch) to produce deliverables **and** turn counts.\n2. `check_runaway <those same tasks>`.\n3. **If it FAILS, the edit is REJECTED** — revert it, give the enforced step a hard cap (it fires a\n   fixed small number of times, then the agent finishes), or pick a different lever; then re-test.\n   **Do not commit a runaway** — the canary will abort the iteration and you'll have spent the budget\n   for nothing.\n\nA prose-only `system_prompt.md` edit can't blow up the loop, so it doesn't need this — but a prose\nedit that *pushes the agent to do much more work* can still inflate turns, so checking is cheap insurance.","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"ad004501-7372-4a84-90be-02a36df6419c","uuid":"dbdedbf5-9de4-4b1e-a0b6-df9732da9b3f","timestamp":"2026-06-27T21:44:01.486Z","tool_use_result":{"stdout":"=== run_harvey_agent.md ===\n# Tool — `run_harvey_agent`\n\n> The mutator invokes this from its shell to run a BATCH of Harvey rollouts concurrently, for triage.\n\n## Purpose\n\nRun the **frozen Harvey agent** on **one or many task ids** (a **batch**, run **concurrently**),\nusing your **current `harness/`**: the broker overlays your edited `system_prompt.md` / `tools.py` /\n`agent_loop.py` onto the bare harvey-labs checkout and runs that (exactly what the standard eval\nwould run), producing a deliverable + trajectory **per task** you can inspect and proxy-grade. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_0144YFh1UsRKqJQkWHFnpK8t","type":"tool_result","content":"=== BARE SYSTEM PROMPT (len 19691) ===\nYou are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.\n\n## Workspace layout\n\nEverything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in\n`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:\n`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.\n\n- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for\n  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at\n  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.\n- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.\n  Read-only.\n- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness\n  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.\n- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the\n  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be\n  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.\n\n## Tool conventions\n\n- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and\n  plain text).\n- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables\n  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).\n- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`\n  summarizing your work.\n- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.\n\nThe skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file\nformats. Read them before tackling the task.\n\n\n## Skill: docx\n\n---\nname: docx\ndescription: \"Use this skill to author, edit, redline, or validate Microsoft Word .docx files. Covers creating new documents from markdown or templates, editing existing documents in place, generating tracked-changes redlines, adding comments, and accepting/rejecting revisions. For READING existing .docx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'draft a memo', 'mark up the agreement', 'redline this', 'add comments to', 'fill the engagement letter template'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .xlsx, .pptx, or .doc (legacy Word).\"\n---\n\n# DOCX authoring, editing, redlining\n\n> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .docx, use the harness `read` tool. It already returns structured text via pandoc. This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *validating*.\n\n## Quick reference\n\n| Goal | Use |\n|---|---|\n| Generate a new doc from markdown | `scripts/generate_from_md.py` (Pandoc + reference template) |\n| Generate a new doc programmatically | `python-docx` directly |\n| Fill a templated agreement | `scripts/template_fill.py` (docxtpl / Jinja) |\n| Edit an existing doc | `scripts/unpack.py` → mutate XML → `scripts/pack.py` |\n| Produce a tracked-changes redline | `scripts/redline.py` |\n| Add comments to a passage | `scripts/comments_add.py` |\n| Accept all redlines | `scripts/accept_changes.py` |\n| Validate a docx before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` (mandatory final step) |\n\nAll scripts live in `workspace/skills/docx/scripts/` once the harness has set up the workspace. Invoke them via `bash`.\n\n## Creating a new document\n\nPick by what you have:\n\n- **Markdown content + a styled firm template** → `generate_from_md.py input.md out.docx template.docx`. Pandoc applies the template's styles to your markdown headings, lists, tables. Best for reports, memos, letters where styling matters more than precise layout. Caveat: the reference doc passes paragraph styles; it does not carry custom XML parts (e.g., comment threads).\n- **A template with named placeholders** → `template_fill.py template.docx context.json out.docx`. docxtpl renders Jinja2 expressions inside the template. Best for engagement letters, NDAs, structured agreements.\n- **Programmatic build** → write Python using `python-docx`. Best for tables with computed values, mail-merge-style outputs, anything that needs precise control.\n\nWhen unsure, prefer the markdown + reference-doc path — Pandoc handles the OOXML correctness so you don't have to.\n\n## Editing an existing document\n\nThree-step pattern:\n\n```bash\npython scripts/unpack.py input.docx workdir/\n# edit XML files under workdir/word/\npython scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.docx\npython scripts/validate.py output.docx\n```\n\nKey files inside the unpacked tree:\n\n- `word/document.xml` — body content (paragraphs, runs, tables)\n- `word/styles.xml` — paragraph + run style definitions\n- `word/numbering.xml` — list-numbering definitions (don't break existing ID references)\n- `word/comments.xml` — comment thread content (created by `comments_add.py`)\n- `word/header*.xml`, `word/footer*.xml` — running headers/footers\n- `[Content_Types].xml` — MIME registration for every part. Edit when you add a new part type.\n- `_rels/` and `word/_rels/` — relationships between parts. Edit when you reference a new image, comment, or external resource.\n\n### Run-merging gotcha\n\nWord writes adjacent runs (`<w:r>`) with identical formatting separately. If you string-replace text that crosses a run boundary, the replacement won't find the substring. `unpack.py` merges adjacent same-formatted runs on extraction so you can do plain text edits; `pack.py` is permissive about whatever run structure you write back.\n\n### Smart-quote escaping\n\nMicrosoft Word uses smart quotes (`\"` `\"` `'` `'`) which must be XML-escaped or written as the actual code points. `unpack.py` substitutes them with XML entities for editing safety; `pack.py` reverses the substitution before zipping. Don't manually re-escape — let the scripts handle it.\n\n### Whitespace preservation\n\nTrailing whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements is significant. Both `unpack.py` and `pack.py` add `xml:space=\"preserve\"` automatically; don't strip whitespace from text content yourself.\n\n## Redlines (tracked changes)\n\n```bash\npython scripts/redline.py original.docx revised.docx redlined.docx \\\n  --author \"Reviewer\" --date \"2026-04-30\"\n```\n\nDefault mode shells out to **Python-Redlines** (MIT) which compares the two documents and emits proper `<w:ins>`/`<w:del>` revision elements. Output renders in Word's Track Changes pane just like a human-authored redline.\n\n### Manual mode\n\nIf `--mode=manual` is passed, the script falls back to a paragraph SequenceMatcher + word-level diff-match-patch pass. Use this when:\n- Python-Redlines fails on a particular doc structure (rare).\n- You want to control which paragraphs are diffed.\n- The change is purely formatting (`<w:rPrChange>` runs) rather than text.\n\n### What gets tracked\n\n- Insertions and deletions of text\n- Insertions and deletions of paragraphs (deleted-whole-paragraph case requires `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>` or you get an empty paragraph after acceptance)\n- Run-property changes via `<w:rPrChange>` (formatting-only revisions)\n\n### What doesn't get tracked\n\n- Table cell additions/removals (Python-Redlines emits the cells as plain edits)\n- Style-definition changes (changes to `styles.xml` aren't revision-trackable)\n- Image swaps\n\n## Comments\n\n```bash\npython scripts/comments_add.py document.docx comments.json\n```\n\n`comments.json` is a list of `{anchor_text, author, comment}` objects. The script:\n- Locates each `anchor_text` in the document body and wraps it with `<w:commentRangeStart>` / `<w:commentRangeEnd>` plus a `<w:commentReference>` run.\n- Creates or appends to `word/comments.xml` (with proper id assignment).\n- Patches `[Content_Types].xml` and `word/_rels/document.xml.rels` if commenting is being added for the first time.\n\nAnchor matching is exact-string. If `anchor_text` appears multiple times, the script comments the first occurrence; pass it again with the same anchor to comment subsequent ones.\n\n`<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` must be siblings of the `<w:r>` runs they bracket — never nested inside a run. If you write comments by hand, follow this rule.\n\n## Accept / reject changes\n\n```bash\npython scripts/accept_changes.py redlined.docx accepted.docx\n```\n\nUses LibreOffice headless via a documented StarBasic macro:\n\n```basic\nSub AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.store() End Sub\n```\n\nLibreOffice must be installed and on PATH (`soffice` binary). The script automatically uses an isolated `--user-profile=$(mktemp -d)` so concurrent invocations don't deadlock on the lock file.\n\nTo reject all changes instead: edit the script to call `RejectAllRedlines()`. To accept selectively: this isn't supported — open the doc in Word.\n\n## Validation gate\n\n**Always run `validate.py` before declaring the task complete.**\n\n```bash\npython scripts/validate.py output.docx\n```\n\nChecks:\n- Round-trip ZIP integrity\n- XML well-formedness for every part\n- Schema validation against ECMA-376 (WordprocessingML) XSDs\n- Content-type registration for every referenced part\n- Relationship consistency (no dangling rIds)\n\nExit code 0 = valid. Non-zero exit code with line-number diagnostics = fix and re-pack.\n\n## Common pitfalls\n\n- **Legacy `.doc` (binary) is not supported.** Convert with `soffice --convert-to docx input.doc` first.\n- **List numbering breaks after edits.** Numbering lives in `word/numbering.xml` keyed by `numId`. If you delete a list, also delete its numId reference; if you reorder, don't change IDs.\n- **Headers and footers are separate parts** (`word/header1.xml`, etc.). Edits to body content don't touch them.\n- **Pandoc reference-doc passes paragraph styles only.** Custom XML parts (comments, tracked changes baseline) are not carried over.\n- **Don't pretty-print whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements.** Pretty-printing breaks runs that depend on exact spacing.\n- **Tables: dual width specs.** Each cell needs both `columnWidths` (in `<w:tblGrid>`) and per-cell `<w:tcW w:type=\"dxa\">`. Percentages (`pct`) render fine in Word but break in Google Docs.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Reading: use the `read` tool.\n- Producing PDFs from .docx: pipe through `soffice --convert-to pdf` after this skill is done.\n- Signing / encryption / DRM.\n- Word macros (`.docm` with VBA).\n\n\n\n## Skill: pptx\n\n---\nname: pptx\ndescription: \"Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft PowerPoint .pptx files. Covers generating decks from scratch (HTML+PptxGenJS, Marp markdown-to-slides, or python-pptx), editing existing decks in place, and validating output. For READING existing .pptx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a deck', 'create slides', 'edit slide N', 'add a chart slide'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .xlsx, or .ppt (legacy).\"\n---\n\n# PPTX authoring and editing\n\n> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .pptx, use the harness `read` tool (markitdown extracts slide text). This skill is for *writing* and *editing*.\n\n## Quick reference\n\n| Goal | Use |\n|---|---|\n| Generate a deck from scratch (HTML/CSS) | `scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js` |\n| Generate a deck from markdown | `scripts/generate_marp.sh` |\n| Build slides programmatically | `python-pptx` directly |\n| Edit a shape on an existing slide | `scripts/edit_shape.py` (JSON patch) |\n| Add or remove a slide | unpack → edit → pack |\n| QA a deck deterministically | `scripts/deterministic_qa.py` |\n| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |\n\n## Generation modalities\n\n**HTML/CSS via PptxGenJS** (preferred for visual fidelity):\n```bash\nnode scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js deck.json out.pptx\n```\n`deck.json` describes slides as a JSON tree; the script invokes PptxGenJS (and html2pptx for HTML inputs) to produce a fully-editable .pptx. Best for branded decks with gradients, custom fonts, complex shapes.\n\n**Markdown via Marp**:\n```bash\nbash scripts/generate_marp.sh deck.md out.pptx\n```\nBest for content-heavy decks (lectures, reports) where markdown is more natural than JSON.\n\n**Programmatic via python-pptx**:\nBest when shapes are computed (e.g., one slide per data row). Requires manual EMU positioning.\n\n## Editing existing decks\n\nThree-step pattern, like docx:\n```bash\npython scripts/unpack.py input.pptx workdir/\n# edit XML files under workdir/ppt/slides/\npython scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.pptx\npython scripts/validate.py output.pptx\n```\n\nFor surgical shape edits without unpacking, use `edit_shape.py`:\n```bash\npython scripts/edit_shape.py input.pptx \\\n  --slide 2 --shape \"Title 1\" --op set_text --value \"New title\"\n```\n\nJSON patch ops: `set_text`, `set_position` (EMU), `set_size`, `recolor`, `delete`.\n\n## OOXML gotchas specific to pptx\n\n- **Use `defusedxml.minidom`, NOT `xml.etree.ElementTree`.** ElementTree corrupts presentation namespaces during round-tripping. `unpack.py` and `pack.py` use minidom; if you write your own XML manipulation, do the same.\n- **EMU units everywhere.** 1 inch = 914400 EMU. Slide positions, sizes, font sizes (in pt × 100) all use derived EMU values.\n- **Placeholders vs free shapes.** Placeholders inherit from slide masters; free shapes don't. Editing a placeholder's text is `<a:t>` content; editing its layout requires master-slide changes.\n- **Don't pretty-print pptx XML on pack.** Whitespace-significant runs (`<a:r>`) break if reformatted. `pack.py` preserves the original whitespace.\n- **Slide cloning is more than a file copy.** Use `unpack` + `pack` — manual file copies miss the rIds in `_rels/` and the `Content_Types.xml` registration.\n\n## Deterministic QA loop\n\nAfter every generation, run:\n\n```bash\npython scripts/thumbnail.py deck.pptx thumbs/   # PDF + JPEGs per slide\npython scripts/deterministic_qa.py deck.pptx > qa.json\n```\n\n`deterministic_qa.py` checks:\n- Shape bounding boxes don't extend past slide edges\n- No two shapes overlap with > 50% area intersection\n- Font sizes ≥ 11pt for body text, ≥ 18pt for titles\n- All placeholders are filled (no `Click to add title` defaults)\n- Bullet lists don't exceed 7 items per slide\n\nOutput is JSON listing each violation with slide number and shape id. Fix violations and re-render.\n\n(A vision-model QA pass is intentionally out of scope for v1. Add `--use-vision` later if deterministic checks miss layout issues.)\n\n## Validation gate\n\n**Always run `validate.py` before declaring done.** Schema-validates against ECMA-376 PresentationML XSDs, checks rId consistency, content-type registration.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Reading: use the `read` tool.\n- SmartArt creation (limited python-pptx support).\n- Complex embedded charts beyond what python-pptx exposes.\n- Slide transitions / animations (rarely matter for legal output).\n- Vision-model layout review (deferred to v2).\n\n\n\n## Skill: xlsx\n\n---\nname: xlsx\ndescription: \"Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft Excel .xlsx files. Covers building workbooks with formulas, editing existing files, recalculating formulas, and scanning for #REF!/#DIV/0!/#VALUE! errors. For READING existing .xlsx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a model', 'create a spreadsheet', 'fill the schedule', 'recalculate'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .pptx, or .xls (legacy Excel).\"\n---\n\n# XLSX authoring and editing\n\n> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .xlsx, use the harness `read` tool (pandas extracts every sheet as a markdown table). This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *recalculating*.\n\n## Quick reference\n\n| Goal | Use |\n|---|---|\n| Build a workbook from scratch | `openpyxl` directly, or `scripts/build_workbook.py` for banker conventions |\n| Edit cells in an existing file | `openpyxl.load_workbook(...)` → mutate → save |\n| Recalculate formulas (full fidelity) | `scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py` |\n| Recalculate formulas (no LibreOffice) | `scripts/recalc_pure_python.py` |\n| Scan for formula errors | `scripts/scan_errors.py` |\n| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |\n\n## Banker conventions (mandatory for financial models)\n\nApply these to every workbook unless the task explicitly overrides:\n\n- **Inputs are blue, formulas are black, cross-sheet references are green, external links are red.** Use `Font(color='0000FF')` etc.\n- **Negatives in parentheses, not minus signs.** Use number format `#,##0;(#,##0)`.\n- **Red negatives in P&L tables.** Use `#,##0;[Red](#,##0)`.\n- **Accounting format for currency.** `_-* #,##0_-;-* #,##0_-;_-* \"-\"_-;_-@_-` (or the localized equivalent).\n- **Multiples shown as `0.0x`**, not `0.0` followed by an \"x\" character. Format: `0.0\"x\"`.\n- **Underline-only on totals**, not bold-and-underline. Use `Border(bottom=Side(style='thin'))`.\n- **No merged cells in input ranges.** Merged cells break formulas that reference them; reserve merging for headers and titles only.\n- **Units in adjacent cells**, not in the cell with the value. `($M)` next to the value, not `\"$1,234M\"` as a string.\n\n`scripts/build_workbook.py` applies these conventions automatically given a JSON spec.\n\n## Formula authoring\n\n- **Always emit formulas, never calculated values.** If the user wants `revenue × growth`, write `=B2*C2`, not `1234.56`. The recalc step materializes values.\n- **Use named ranges** for cross-sheet inputs. `wb.defined_names[\"assumptions\"] = DefinedName(...)`. Easier to audit.\n- **Document units in adjacent cells** so the model is self-explanatory.\n- **No volatile functions in hot paths.** `OFFSET`, `INDIRECT`, `NOW`, `TODAY` recalculate on every change and slow large workbooks.\n\n## Recalculation — choose your engine\n\n`openpyxl` writes formula *strings*; it does not evaluate them. You must recalculate before delivery, otherwise consumers will see `=B2*C2` literal text where they expect numbers (in some readers) or stale cached values (in others).\n\n**LibreOffice path** (`recalc_libreoffice.py`) — ground truth:\n```bash\npython scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py input.xlsx output.xlsx\n```\nDrives LibreOffice headless via the StarBasic macro `ThisComponent.calculateAll(); ThisComponent.store()`. Slow (~5–10s per workbook) but matches Excel for nearly every function. Use this when the workbook contains modern Excel features.\n\n**Pure-Python path** (`recalc_pure_python.py`) — fast, partial:\n```bash\npython scripts/recalc_pure_python.py input.xlsx output.xlsx\n```\nUses `xlcalculator` to evaluate every formula in pure Python. Fast (~0.5s per workbook). Covers ~80% of common functions: arithmetic, `SUM`, `IF`, `VLOOKUP`, `INDEX`/`MATCH`, basic string/date functions.\n\n**Does NOT support**: `XLOOKUP`, `LET`, dynamic arrays (`FILTER`, `SEQUENCE`, `UNIQUE`), `LAMBDA`, `BYROW`, `TEXTJOIN` with refs, structured table references, most modern (post-2019) Excel features.\n\nIf you used any of those, run the LibreOffice path. The pure-Python path is for CI environments without LibreOffice.\n\n## Error scan\n\nAfter every recalc, scan for formula errors:\n\n```bash\npython scripts/scan_errors.py output.xlsx > errors.json\n```\n\nReports every cell whose computed value matches `#REF!`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`, `#NAME?`, `#NULL!`, `#NUM!`, or `#N/A`. Output is JSONL with `{sheet, address, value}` per line.\n\nIf errors exist, fix and re-recalc. Don't ship a workbook with `#REF!`s — it's the most common reason a deliverable fails QA.\n\n## Validation gate\n\n`scripts/validate.py output.xlsx` schema-validates against ECMA-376 SpreadsheetML XSDs and confirms ZIP integrity, content-type registration, sheet relationships.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Reading: use the `read` tool.\n- **PivotTables** — `openpyxl` round-trips existing pivots but cannot create or modify them. If a task requires pivot creation, escalate (Windows-only via COM, not portable).\n- **DAX measures and Power Pivot** — `openpyxl` can't write these. Same limitation.\n- **VBA macros (`.xlsm`)** — out of scope for this skill. Macros require Excel runtime.\n- **Conditional formatting beyond simple cell-value rules** — `openpyxl` supports basic CF; complex rules (top-N, data bars across sheets) often fail to round-trip.\n- **Charts beyond the python-pptx-equivalent set** — line/bar/scatter work; combo charts and trendlines round-trip unreliably.","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"ad004501-7372-4a84-90be-02a36df6419c","uuid":"157b58c1-9b22-4d32-8fad-2731fd5079a0","timestamp":"2026-06-27T21:47:39.912Z","tool_use_result":{"stdout":"=== BARE SYSTEM PROMPT (len 19691) ===\nYou are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.\n\n## Workspace layout\n\nEverything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in\n`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:\n`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.\n\n- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for\n  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at\n  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.\n- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.\n  Read-only.\n- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness\n  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.\n- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the\n  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be\n  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.\n\n## Tool conventions\n\n- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and\n  plain text).\n- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables\n  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).\n- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`\n  summarizing your work.\n- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.\n\nThe skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file\nformats. Read them before tackling the task.\n\n\n## Skill: docx\n\n---\nname: docx\ndescription: \"Use this skill to author, edit, redline, or validate Microsoft Word .docx files. Covers creating new documents from markdown or templates, editing existing documents in place, generating tracked-changes redlines, adding comments, and accepting/rejecting revisions. For READING existing .docx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'draft a memo', 'mark up the agreement', 'redline this', 'add comments to', 'fill the engagement letter template'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .xlsx, .pptx, or .doc (legacy Word).\"\n---\n\n# DOCX authoring, editing, redlining\n\n> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .docx, use the harness `read` tool. It already returns structured text via pandoc. This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *validating*.\n\n## Quick reference\n\n| Goal | Use |\n|---|---|\n| Generate a new doc from markdown | `scripts/generate_from_md.py` (Pandoc + reference template) |\n| Generate a new doc programmatically | `python-docx` directly |\n| Fill a templated agreement | `scripts/template_fill.py` (docxtpl / Jinja) |\n| Edit an existing doc | `scripts/unpack.py` → mutate XML → `scripts/pack.py` |\n| Produce a tracked-changes redline | `scripts/redline.py` |\n| Add comments to a passage | `scripts/comments_add.py` |\n| Accept all redlines | `scripts/accept_changes.py` |\n| Validate a docx before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` (mandatory final step) |\n\nAll scripts live in `workspace/skills/docx/scripts/` once the harness has set up the workspace. Invoke them via `bash`.\n\n## Creating a new document\n\nPick by what you have:\n\n- **Markdown content + a styled firm template** → `generate_from_md.py input.md out.docx template.docx`. Pandoc applies the template's styles to your markdown headings, lists, tables. Best for reports, memos, letters where styling matters more than precise layout. Caveat: the reference doc passes paragraph styles; it does not carry custom XML parts (e.g., comment threads).\n- **A template with named placeholders** → `template_fill.py template.docx context.json out.docx`. docxtpl renders Jinja2 expressions inside the template. Best for engagement letters, NDAs, structured agreements.\n- **Programmatic build** → write Python using `python-docx`. Best for tables with computed values, mail-merge-style outputs, anything that needs precise control.\n\nWhen unsure, prefer the markdown + reference-doc path — Pandoc handles the OOXML correctness so you don't have to.\n\n## Editing an existing document\n\nThree-step pattern:\n\n```bash\npython scripts/unpack.py input.docx workdir/\n# edit XML files under workdir/word/\npython scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.docx\npython scripts/validate.py output.docx\n```\n\nKey files inside the unpacked tree:\n\n- `word/document.xml` — body content (paragraphs, runs, tables)\n- `word/styles.xml` — paragraph + run style definitions\n- `word/numbering.xml` — list-numbering definitions (don't break existing ID references)\n- `word/comments.xml` — comment thread content (created by `comments_add.py`)\n- `word/header*.xml`, `word/footer*.xml` — running headers/footers\n- `[Content_Types].xml` — MIME registration for every part. Edit when you add a new part type.\n- `_rels/` and `word/_rels/` — relationships between parts. Edit when you reference a new image, comment, or external resource.\n\n### Run-merging gotcha\n\nWord writes adjacent runs (`<w:r>`) with identical formatting separately. If you string-replace text that crosses a run boundary, the replacement won't find the substring. `unpack.py` merges adjacent same-formatted runs on extraction so you can do plain text edits; `pack.py` is permissive about whatever run structure you write back.\n\n### Smart-quote escaping\n\nMicrosoft Word uses smart quotes (`\"` `\"` `'` `'`) which must be XML-escaped or written as the actual code points. `unpack.py` substitutes them with XML entities for editing safety; `pack.py` reverses the substitution before zipping. Don't manually re-escape — let the scripts handle it.\n\n### Whitespace preservation\n\nTrailing whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements is significant. Both `unpack.py` and `pack.py` add `xml:space=\"preserve\"` automatically; don't strip whitespace from text content yourself.\n\n## Redlines (tracked changes)\n\n```bash\npython scripts/redline.py original.docx revised.docx redlined.docx \\\n  --author \"Reviewer\" --date \"2026-04-30\"\n```\n\nDefault mode shells out to **Python-Redlines** (MIT) which compares the two documents and emits proper `<w:ins>`/`<w:del>` revision elements. Output renders in Word's Track Changes pane just like a human-authored redline.\n\n### Manual mode\n\nIf `--mode=manual` is passed, the script falls back to a paragraph SequenceMatcher + word-level diff-match-patch pass. Use this when:\n- Python-Redlines fails on a particular doc structure (rare).\n- You want to control which paragraphs are diffed.\n- The change is purely formatting (`<w:rPrChange>` runs) rather than text.\n\n### What gets tracked\n\n- Insertions and deletions of text\n- Insertions and deletions of paragraphs (deleted-whole-paragraph case requires `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>` or you get an empty paragraph after acceptance)\n- Run-property changes via `<w:rPrChange>` (formatting-only revisions)\n\n### What doesn't get tracked\n\n- Table cell additions/removals (Python-Redlines emits the cells as plain edits)\n- Style-definition changes (changes to `styles.xml` aren't revision-trackable)\n- Image swaps\n\n## Comments\n\n```bash\npython scripts/comments_add.py document.docx comments.json\n```\n\n`comments.json` is a list of `{anchor_text, author, comment}` objects. The script:\n- Locates each `anchor_text` in the document body and wraps it with `<w:commentRangeStart>` / `<w:commentRangeEnd>` plus a `<w:commentReference>` run.\n- Creates or appends to `word/comments.xml` (with proper id assignment).\n- Patches `[Content_Types].xml` and `word/_rels/document.xml.rels` if commenting is being added for the first time.\n\nAnchor matching is exact-string. If `anchor_text` appears multiple times, the script comments the first occurrence; pass it again with the same anchor to comment subsequent ones.\n\n`<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` must be siblings of the `<w:r>` runs they bracket — never nested inside a run. If you write comments by hand, follow this rule.\n\n## Accept / reject changes\n\n```bash\npython scripts/accept_changes.py redlined.docx accepted.docx\n```\n\nUses LibreOffice headless via a documented StarBasic macro:\n\n```basic\nSub AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.store() End Sub\n```\n\nLibreOffice must be installed and on PATH (`soffice` binary). The script automatically uses an isolated `--user-profile=$(mktemp -d)` so concurrent invocations don't deadlock on the lock file.\n\nTo reject all changes instead: edit the script to call `RejectAllRedlines()`. To accept selectively: this isn't supported — open the doc in Word.\n\n## Validation gate\n\n**Always run `validate.py` before declaring the task complete.**\n\n```bash\npython scripts/validate.py output.docx\n```\n\nChecks:\n- Round-trip ZIP integrity\n- XML well-formedness for every part\n- Schema validation against ECMA-376 (WordprocessingML) XSDs\n- Content-type registration for every referenced part\n- Relationship consistency (no dangling rIds)\n\nExit code 0 = valid. Non-zero exit code with line-number diagnostics = fix and re-pack.\n\n## Common pitfalls\n\n- **Legacy `.doc` (binary) is not supported.** Convert with `soffice --convert-to docx input.doc` first.\n- **List numbering breaks after edits.** Numbering lives in `word/numbering.xml` keyed by `numId`. If you delete a list, also delete its numId reference; if you reorder, don't change IDs.\n- **Headers and footers are separate parts** (`word/header1.xml`, etc.). Edits to body content don't touch them.\n- **Pandoc reference-doc passes paragraph styles only.** Custom XML parts (comments, tracked changes baseline) are not carried over.\n- **Don't pretty-print whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements.** Pretty-printing breaks runs that depend on exact spacing.\n- **Tables: dual width specs.** Each cell needs both `columnWidths` (in `<w:tblGrid>`) and per-cell `<w:tcW w:type=\"dxa\">`. Percentages (`pct`) render fine in Word but break in Google Docs.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Reading: use the `read` tool.\n- Producing PDFs from .docx: pipe through `soffice --convert-to pdf` after this skill is done.\n- Signing / encryption / DRM.\n- Word macros (`.docm` with VBA).\n\n\n\n## Skill: pptx\n\n---\nname: pptx\ndescription: \"Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft PowerPoint .pptx files. Covers generating decks from scratch (HTML+PptxGenJS, Marp markdown-to-slides, or python-pptx), editing existing decks in place, and validating output. For READING existing .pptx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a deck', 'create slides', 'edit slide N', 'add a chart slide'. For READING existing .xlsx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a model', 'create a spreadsheet', 'fill the schedule', 'recalculate'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .pptx, or .xls (legacy Excel).\"\n---\n\n# XLSX authoring and editing\n\n> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .xlsx, use the harness `read` tool (pandas extracts every sheet as a markdown table). This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *recalculating*.\n\n## Quick reference\n\n| Goal | Use |\n|---|---|\n| Build a workbook from scratch | `openpyxl` directly, or `scripts/build_workbook.py` for banker conventions |\n| Edit cells in an existing file | `openpyxl.load_workbook(...)` → mutate → save |\n| Recalculate formulas (full fidelity) | `scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py` |\n| Recalculate formulas (no LibreOffice) | `scripts/recalc_pure_python.py` |\n| Scan for formula errors | `scripts/scan_errors.py` |\n| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |\n\n## Banker conventions (mandatory for financial models)\n\nApply these to every workbook unless the task explicitly overrides:\n\n- **Inputs are blue, formulas are black, cross-sheet references are green, external links are red.** Use `Font(color='0000FF')` etc.\n- **Negatives in parentheses, not minus signs.** Use number format `#,##0;(#,##0)`.\n- **Red negatives in P&L tables.** Use `#,##0;[Red](#,##0)`.\n- **Accounting format for currency.** `_-* #,##0_-;-* #,##0_-;_-* \"-\"_-;_-@_-` (or the localized equivalent).\n- **Multiples shown as `0.0x`**, not `0.0` followed by an \"x\" character. Format: `0.0\"x\"`.\n- **Underline-only on totals**, not bold-and-underline. Use `Border(bottom=Side(style='thin'))`.\n- **No merged cells in input ranges.** Merged cells break formulas that reference them; reserve merging for headers and titles only.\n- **Units in adjacent cells**, not in the cell with the value. `($M)` next to the value, not `\"$1,234M\"` as a string.\n\n`scripts/build_workbook.py` applies these conventions automatically given a JSON spec.\n\n## Formula authoring\n\n- **Always emit formulas, never calculated values.** If the user wants `revenue × growth`, write `=B2*C2`, not `1234.56`. The recalc step materializes values.\n- **Use named ranges** for cross-sheet inputs. `wb.defined_names[\"assumptions\"] = DefinedName(...)`. Easier to audit.\n- **Document units in adjacent cells** so the model is self-explanatory.\n- **No volatile functions in hot paths.** `OFFSET`, `INDIRECT`, `NOW`, `TODAY` recalculate on every change and slow large workbooks.\n\n## Recalculation — choose your engine\n\n`openpyxl` writes formula *strings*; it does not evaluate them. You must recalculate before delivery, otherwise consumers will see `=B2*C2` literal text where they expect numbers (in some readers) or stale cached values (in others).\n\n**LibreOffice path** (`recalc_libreoffice.py`) — ground truth:\n```bash\npython scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py input.xlsx output.xlsx\n```\nDrives LibreOffice headless via the StarBasic macro `ThisComponent.calculateAll(); ThisComponent.store()`. Slow (~5–10s per workbook) but matches Excel for nearly every function. Use this when the workbook contains modern Excel features.\n\n**Pure-Python path** (`recalc_pure_python.py`) — fast, partial:\n```bash\npython scripts/recalc_pure_python.py input.xlsx output.xlsx\n```\nUses `xlcalculator` to evaluate every formula in pure Python. Fast (~0.5s per workbook). Covers ~80% of common functions: arithmetic, `SUM`, `IF`, `VLOOKUP`, `INDEX`/`MATCH`, basic string/date functions.\n\n**Does NOT support**: `XLOOKUP`, `LET`, dynamic arrays (`FILTER`, `SEQUENCE`, `UNIQUE`), `LAMBDA`, `BYROW`, `TEXTJOIN` with refs, structured table references, most modern (post-2019) Excel features.\n\nIf you used any of those, run the LibreOffice path. The pure-Python path is for CI environments without LibreOffice.\n\n## Error scan\n\nAfter every recalc, scan for formula errors:\n\n```bash\npython scripts/scan_errors.py output.xlsx > errors.json\n```\n\nReports every cell whose computed value matches `#REF!`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`, `#NAME?`, `#NULL!`, `#NUM!`, or `#N/A`. Output is JSONL with `{sheet, address, value}` per line.\n\nIf errors exist, fix and re-recalc. Don't ship a workbook with `#REF!`s — it's the most common reason a deliverable fails QA.\n\n## Validation gate\n\n`scripts/validate.py output.xlsx` schema-validates against ECMA-376 SpreadsheetML XSDs and confirms ZIP integrity, content-type registration, sheet relationships.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Reading: use the `read` tool.\n- **PivotTables** — `openpyxl` round-trips existing pivots but cannot create or modify them. If a task requires pivot creation, escalate (Windows-only via COM, not portable).\n- **DAX measures and Power Pivot** — `openpyxl` can't write these. Same limitation.\n- **VBA macros (`.xlsm`)** — out of scope for this skill. Macros require Excel runtime.\n- **Conditional formatting beyond simple cell-value rules** — `openpyxl` supports basic CF; complex rules (top-N, data bars across sheets) often fail to round-trip.\n- **Charts beyond the python-pptx-equivalent set** — line/bar/scatter work; combo charts and trendlines round-trip unreliably.","stderr":"","interrupted":false,"isImage":false,"noOutputExpected":false}}
